| Max awaiting client action                | 900s    | `runtime.maxAwaitingClientActionSeconds`           | Yes                | §7.3           |
| Max elicitation wait                      | 600s    | `runtime.maxElicitationWaitSeconds`               | Yes (per pool)     | §9.1           |
| Max elicitations/session                  | 50      | `runtime.maxElicitationsPerSession`               | Yes (per pool)     | §9.1           |
| Max request_input wait                    | 600s    | `runtime.maxRequestInputWaitSeconds`              | Yes (per pool)     | §8.8           |
| gRPC keepalive interval (gateway→pod)     | 10s     | `grpc.keepaliveTimeMs` (10000)                    | Yes                | §10.1          |
| gRPC keepalive timeout (gateway→pod)      | 5s      | `grpc.keepaliveTimeoutMs` (5000)                  | Yes                | §10.1          |
| Coordinator hold timeout                  | 120s    | `adapter.coordinatorHoldTimeoutSeconds`           | Yes                | §10.1          |
| Dual-store unavailability max             | 60s     | `gateway.dualStoreUnavailableMaxSeconds`          | Yes                | §10.1          |
| CoordinatorFence RPC timeout              | 5s      | (hard-coded; not configurable in v1)              | No                 | §10.1          |
| Checkpoint barrier ack timeout            | 90s     | `gateway.checkpointBarrierAckTimeoutSeconds`      | Yes                | §10.1          |
| Elicitation per-hop forwarding timeout    | 30s     | (hard-coded; not configurable in v1)              | No                 | §9.1           |
| External interceptor default timeout      | 500ms   | `interceptor.timeoutMs` (per registration)        | Yes (per interceptor) | §4.8        |
| PreLLMRequest/PostLLMResponse interceptor | 100ms   | `interceptor.timeoutMs` (per registration)        | Yes (per interceptor) | §4.8        |
| Connector interceptor default timeout     | 200ms   | `interceptor.timeoutMs` (per registration)        | Yes (per interceptor) | §4.8        |
| Credential lease cert expiry warning      | 1h      | `credentials.expiryWarningLeadSeconds` (3600)     | Yes                | §4.9           |
| Admission webhook timeout                 | 5s      | `admissionWebhook.timeoutSeconds`                 | Yes                | §13.1          |
| DNS resolution timeout (pod egress)       | 5s      | CoreDNS `forward` plugin `policy` timeout         | Yes (cluster-level)| §13.2          |
| Max `created` state lifetime              | 300s    | `gateway.maxCreatedStateTimeoutSeconds`            | Yes                | §15.1          |
| Max `finalizing` state lifetime           | 600s    | `gateway.maxFinalizingTimeoutSeconds`              | Yes                | §6.2           |
| Max `ready` state lifetime                | 300s    | `gateway.maxReadyTimeoutSeconds`                   | Yes                | §6.2           |
| Max `starting` state lifetime             | 120s    | `gateway.maxStartingTimeoutSeconds`                | Yes                | §6.2           |
| Rate-limit fail-open window               | 60s     | `gateway.rateLimitFailOpenMaxSeconds`              | Yes                | §12.4          |
| Quota fail-open cumulative max            | 300s    | `gateway.quotaFailOpenCumulativeMaxSeconds`        | Yes                | §12.4          |
| Delegation usage quiescence timeout       | 5s      | `delegation.usageQuiescenceTimeoutSeconds`         | Yes                | §8.10          |
| Delegation cascade timeout                | 3600s   | `delegation.cascadeTimeoutSeconds`                 | Yes                | §8.10          |
| Delegation max tree recovery              | 600s    | `delegation.maxTreeRecoverySeconds`                | Yes                | §8.10          |
| Delegation max level recovery             | 120s    | `delegation.maxLevelRecoverySeconds`               | Yes                | §8.10          |
| Credential lease TTL                      | 3600s   | `credentials.leaseTTLSeconds` (per pool)           | Yes (per pool)     | §4.9           |
| SandboxClaim orphan timeout               | 300s    | `--claim-orphan-timeout`                            | Yes                | §4.6.1         |
| Billing stream TTL                        | 3600s   | `billing.streamTTLSeconds`                          | Yes                | §11.2.1        |
| Legal hold checkpoint reconciler interval | 900s    | (hard-coded; not configurable in v1)              | No                 | §12.8          |
| `task_complete_acknowledged` timeout       | 30s     | (hard-coded; not configurable in v1)              | No                 | §4.7           |
| Max suspended pod hold                    | 900s    | `gateway.maxSuspendedPodHoldSeconds`               | Yes (deploy + tenant, most restrictive wins) | §6.2 |
| Delegation budget key TTL                 | 259200s | `delegation.budgetKeyTTLSeconds`                   | Yes                | §8.3           |

Cancellation is first-class: clients and parent agents can cancel sessions/tasks cleanly.

**`maxRequestInputWait` — inter-agent blocking timeout.** `lenny/request_input` is an inter-agent blocking call (distinct from `lenny/request_elicitation`, which is the human-facing tool governed by `maxElicitationWait`). When a child agent calls `lenny/request_input`, it blocks in `input_required` sub-state until a peer responds via `lenny/send_message` with a matching `inReplyTo`. `maxRequestInputWaitSeconds` (default: 600s, configurable per pool in the `limits:` block of the RuntimeDefinition — same placement as `maxElicitationWait`) caps this wait. When the timeout fires, the gateway resolves the blocked tool call with a `REQUEST_INPUT_TIMEOUT` error delivered as a normal tool-call error to the runtime; the child can then handle it (retry, fall back, or fail). The `maxSessionAge` timer continues running during `input_required` — `maxRequestInputWaitSeconds` provides an earlier, dedicated bound. This timeout is distinct from `maxElicitationWait` and both may be configured independently. When a `request_input` times out, the gateway emits a `request_input_expired` event on the parent's `lenny/await_children` stream: `{ "type": "request_input_expired", "childId": "...", "requestId": "...", "expiredAt": "<ISO8601>" }`, allowing the parent to distinguish "child's input request timed out" from "child expired for other reasons."

**Session expiry warning:** The gateway sends a `session_expiring_soon` event to the client and pod 5 minutes before `maxSessionAge` expires. This gives the agent time to checkpoint and the client time to extend or wrap up. The agent receives a `DEADLINE_APPROACHING` signal via the adapter.

> **Note:** The 2h default is a conservative starting point. Deployers should tune `maxSessionAge` per Runtime based on expected workload duration. There is no platform-imposed upper bound — certificate renewal and SA token refresh are handled transparently at any session duration (see §10.3).

### 11.4 User Invalidation

Three levels:

| Level        | Effect                                                                           |
| ------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Soft disable | Deny new sessions                                                                |
| Hard disable | Also block new delegated tasks; reject pending delegation approvals for the user |
| Full revoke  | Terminate active sessions, invalidate cached auth, deny reconnects               |

**Full revoke propagation mechanism:**

1. Gateway looks up all active sessions for the user (via SessionStore).
2. For each session in the user's task tree: gateway sends a `Terminate` RPC to the pod with reason `USER_REVOKED`.
3. The pod's runtime adapter initiates graceful shutdown (SIGTERM to agent, wait up to 10s, then SIGKILL).
4. Gateway marks all sessions as terminated in SessionStore.
5. Cached auth tokens for the user are invalidated in Redis.
6. Credential leases held by the user's sessions are revoked (returned to pool).
7. Pending elicitations for the user are dismissed.

> **Note:** Invalidation is asynchronous — the API call returns immediately and propagation completes within seconds. The `GET /v1/sessions` endpoint reflects the updated state once propagation completes.

### 11.5 Idempotency

Critical operations support idempotency keys:

- CreateSession
- FinalizeWorkspace
- StartSession
- SpawnChild
- Approve/DenyDelegation
- Resume

**Mechanism.** Clients provide an idempotency key via the `Idempotency-Key` HTTP header (for REST) or the `idempotencyKey` field in the MCP tool input (for MCP). The key is an opaque string of at most 128 characters; UUIDs are recommended. The gateway stores the key, request hash, and response in Postgres (table `idempotency_keys`, keyed by `(tenant_id, idempotency_key)`) with a TTL of 24 hours. Within this window, a duplicate request with the same key returns the cached response (same HTTP status and body) without re-executing the operation. If the same key is reused with a different request body (detected via SHA-256 hash comparison), the gateway returns `422 IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_REUSED` to prevent accidental misuse. After the 24-hour window, the key record is garbage-collected and the key may be reused. Idempotency keys are scoped per tenant — the same key string used by different tenants is treated independently.

Prevents duplicate sessions or children during gateway failover or client retries.

### 11.6 Circuit Breakers

**Two types of circuit breaker exist in the platform.** (1) **Automatic, per-subsystem circuit breakers** — in-memory, managed by each gateway replica, triggered by consecutive failure thresholds (e.g., the gateway subsystem breakers in Section 4.1, the Token Service breaker in Section 4.3, and the KMS signing breaker in Section 10.2). These are not operator-controlled and reset automatically via half-open probes. (2) **Operator-managed circuit breakers** — Redis-backed, declared and toggled via the admin API, described in the remainder of this section. The two types are independent: an operator-managed breaker does not affect automatic subsystem breakers, and vice versa.

Operators can declare degraded states:

- Runtime X degraded / offline
- Pool Y full
- External connector Z down
- Uploads temporarily disabled
- Delegation depth > N disabled during incident

**Storage and propagation.** Circuit breaker state is stored in Redis (the Quota/Rate Limiting instance, §12.4) as a key per circuit name: `cb:{name}` → `{state: "open"|"closed", reason: string, opened_at: ISO8601, opened_by: user_id}`. State changes are published on the `circuit_breaker_events` pub/sub channel so all gateway replicas pick up the change within one round-trip (~1ms). Gateway replicas also re-read all circuit states from Redis at startup and cache in-process. The cache TTL is 5 seconds; replicas poll Redis every 5 seconds to detect out-of-band changes (e.g., a Redis write that missed the pub/sub channel due to a reconnect).

**Admin API.** Circuit breakers are managed via the admin API (requires `platform-admin` role):

| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `GET` | `/v1/admin/circuit-breakers` | List all circuit breakers and their current state |
| `GET` | `/v1/admin/circuit-breakers/{name}` | Get state for a single circuit breaker |
| `POST` | `/v1/admin/circuit-breakers/{name}/open` | Open (activate) a circuit breaker. Body: `{ "reason": string }` |
| `POST` | `/v1/admin/circuit-breakers/{name}/close` | Close (deactivate) a circuit breaker |

**AdmissionController evaluation.** The gateway evaluates all active (open) circuit breakers at the start of every session-creation and delegation admission check, before quota and policy evaluation. If any open circuit breaker applies to the requested runtime, pool, connector, or operation type, the request is rejected immediately with `CIRCUIT_BREAKER_OPEN` (HTTP 503, `retryable: false`). The error body includes `circuit_name`, `reason`, and `opened_at`.

**Audit events.** Every state change emits a `circuit_breaker.state_changed` audit event containing: `circuit_name`, `old_state`, `new_state`, `reason`, `operator_user_id`, `timestamp`. These events are written to the append-only audit tables (§11.7).

**Observability.** `lenny_circuit_breaker_open` gauge (labeled by `circuit_name`) is 1 when the named breaker is open, 0 when closed. Alert `CircuitBreakerActive` (severity: warning) fires when any breaker has been open for more than 5 minutes without a close action.

### 11.7 Audit Logging

Every session/task/delegation produces durable records:

- Who requested it (user_id, tenant_id)
- What runtime, pool, isolation profile
- Parent/child lineage
- Which policies were applied and their results
- Token usage
- Retries, cancellations, failures
- External tool access and auth prompts
- Denial reasons

**Integrity:** Audit tables use **append-only semantics** — the gateway's database role (`lenny_app`) has INSERT-only grants on audit tables (no UPDATE, DELETE). These grants are defined in the schema migration files and enforced through the following layered integrity controls:

1. **Startup grant verification (hard-fail in production):** At startup, the gateway queries `information_schema.role_table_grants` and checks that no UPDATE or DELETE grants exist on audit tables for the `lenny_app` role. In production mode (`LENNY_ENV=production`), any unexpected grant causes the gateway to **refuse to start** (fatal error). In non-production environments, a warning is logged instead. This prevents a compromised or misconfigured database from silently undermining audit integrity. Note: startup verification alone is insufficient — a superuser can grant UPDATE/DELETE access after startup and revoke it before the next periodic check. Items 2 and 5 (below) close this window.

   **Trigger-enabled check:** The startup grant verification additionally checks that all billing and audit integrity triggers are enabled: the gateway queries `SELECT tgname, tgenabled FROM pg_trigger WHERE tgname IN ('lenny_tenant_guard', 'lenny_billing_immutability', 'lenny_audit_immutability')` and fails startup (fatal error in production) if any trigger has `tgenabled = 'D'` (disabled). A superuser can `ALTER TABLE ... DISABLE TRIGGER` to bypass these protections without revoking grants. This check is also performed by the `lenny-preflight` Job (see Section 17.6) and by the periodic background integrity check (item 2).

2. **Periodic background integrity check:** A background goroutine re-verifies audit table grants at a configurable interval. The default interval is profile-dependent:
   - **Regulated profiles** (`complianceProfile` in `{soc2, fedramp, hipaa}`): `audit.grantCheckInterval` defaults to **60 seconds** and may not be set higher than 120 seconds via config (the gateway clamps the value at startup with a fatal error if the configured value exceeds the maximum for the active profile).
   - **Unregulated profiles** (`complianceProfile: none`): `audit.grantCheckInterval` defaults to **5 minutes** (300 seconds), configurable up to 15 minutes.
   If drift is detected at runtime (e.g., a superuser added UPDATE grants after startup), the gateway emits a critical alert, increments the `lenny_audit_grant_drift_total` Prometheus counter, and — if `audit.hardFailOnDrift` is enabled — initiates a graceful shutdown. This reduces the detection window to ≤60 seconds for regulated deployments, narrowing the tamper-and-revert window described above.

3. **Hash chaining:** Each audit log entry includes a `prev_hash` column containing the SHA-256 hash of the previous entry's `(id, prev_hash, tenant_id, event_type, payload, created_at)` tuple. The first entry in each tenant partition uses a **per-tenant genesis hash** derived from a random nonce generated at tenant creation time: `genesis_hash = SHA-256("lenny-audit-genesis" || tenant_id || genesis_nonce)`. The `genesis_nonce` (256-bit, generated via `crypto/rand`) is stored in the tenant configuration record and also written to the SIEM (when configured) alongside the first audit event. This ensures that an adversary who reconstructs a hash chain from the genesis point forward must possess the tenant-specific nonce — a well-known fixed genesis hash would allow undetectable chain reconstruction by a database superuser. This creates a tamper-evident chain — any retroactive modification or deletion of an entry breaks the chain for all subsequent entries. The periodic background check (item 2) also samples random chain segments and verifies hash continuity; a broken chain triggers the same critical alert path.

4. **SIEM connectivity validation (optional):** For deployments that require compliance-grade audit integrity, audit events **should** be streamed to an external immutable log (SIEM, cloud audit service, or append-only object storage) in addition to Postgres storage. SIEM is not required — Lenny operates fully without it, with the integrity trade-offs documented below. When `audit.siem.endpoint` is configured, the gateway validates SIEM connectivity at startup: a test event is sent and the gateway **refuses to start** until acknowledgement is received. At runtime, a health check monitors SIEM delivery success rate; if the failure rate exceeds the configured threshold (`audit.siem.failureThresholdPercent`, default 5%), the gateway emits a critical alert and the `/healthz` endpoint reports degraded status. This ensures audit integrity even if the database is compromised, and prevents silent SIEM delivery failures from creating gaps in the external audit trail.

5. **pgaudit logging to external append-only sink.** To close the residual tamper window that remains between periodic grant checks (items 1–2), regulated deployments **must** enable `pgaudit` on the Postgres cluster and stream DDL/GRANT events to an external append-only sink. Configuration:
   - **`audit.pgaudit.enabled`** (bool, default `false`): when `true`, the gateway's startup preflight check (Section 17.6) validates that the `pgaudit` extension is installed and that `pgaudit.log` includes `DDL` and `ROLE` classes. If validation fails in production mode with a regulated `complianceProfile`, the gateway refuses to start.
   - **`audit.pgaudit.sinkEndpoint`** (string): the external append-only endpoint (e.g., a cloud audit logging service, SIEM, or append-only S3 bucket with object lock) to which `pgaudit` log records are forwarded. The gateway does not ship the pgaudit records itself — this is a Postgres-level configuration (e.g., `pg_log` forwarded via a log shipper such as Fluentd/Fluent Bit). The gateway's startup check verifies that the Postgres cluster's `log_destination` configuration includes the configured shipper endpoint.
   - **Regulated tenant requirement:** When any active tenant has a regulated `complianceProfile`, `audit.pgaudit.enabled` must be `true` and `audit.pgaudit.sinkEndpoint` must be configured. The gateway enforces this at startup (fatal error if not satisfied in production mode) and at regulated tenant creation/update (HTTP 422 `COMPLIANCE_PGAUDIT_REQUIRED` if not configured).
   - **What pgaudit captures:** Any `GRANT`, `REVOKE`, or DDL statement on audit tables executed by any database role — including superusers — is logged with role identity, timestamp, and statement text. Because these records are written to the external append-only sink (not Postgres), a superuser who grants UPDATE access and then revokes it leaves an indelible trace in the sink, even if no periodic check cycle fired in the interim.
   - **Metric:** `lenny_pgaudit_grant_events_total` (counter, labeled by `statement_type`: `GRANT`, `REVOKE`, `DDL`) tracks pgaudit-sourced events forwarded to the sink. Alert: `PgAuditSinkDeliveryFailed` fires if the log shipper reports delivery failures for more than `audit.pgaudit.sinkFailureAlertMinutes` (default: 5) minutes.

6. **Erasure role and immutability trigger bypass.** The GDPR erasure job (Section 12.8) must UPDATE billing events (pseudonymization) and DELETE audit events, which conflicts with `lenny_app`'s INSERT-only grants and the `lenny_billing_immutability` / `lenny_audit_immutability` triggers. A dedicated `lenny_erasure` database role resolves this:
   - **Grants:** `lenny_erasure` has `UPDATE` on billing tables (scoped to the `user_id` and free-text PII columns only — not `sequence_number`, `tenant_id`, or cost dimensions), `DELETE` on audit tables, and `INSERT` on audit tables restricted to erasure receipt rows (`event_type LIKE 'gdpr.%'`) via a row-level `BEFORE INSERT` trigger that rejects any `lenny_erasure` INSERT where `event_type NOT LIKE 'gdpr.%'`. It has no `SELECT` beyond what erasure verification requires, or any grants on non-erasure tables. These grants are defined in the schema migration alongside the `lenny_app` grants.
   - **Usage:** Only the erasure background job connects as `lenny_erasure`. Gateway replicas and all other services connect as `lenny_app` and never hold `lenny_erasure` credentials.
   - **Immutability trigger bypass:** The `lenny_billing_immutability` and `lenny_audit_immutability` trigger functions check `current_setting('lenny.erasure_mode', true)`. When the setting is `'true'`, the trigger allows the UPDATE or DELETE and returns without raising an exception. The erasure job sets `SET LOCAL lenny.erasure_mode = 'true'` at the start of each erasure transaction — `SET LOCAL` scopes the setting to the current transaction only, so it cannot leak to other connections via the connection pooler. The startup grant verification (item 1) and periodic background check (item 2) verify that `lenny_app` does not hold UPDATE or DELETE grants; the `lenny_erasure` role's grants are expected and excluded from drift detection.
   - **Startup verification extension:** The trigger-enabled check (item 1) additionally verifies that the `lenny.erasure_mode` guard clause is present in both immutability trigger function bodies (by inspecting `pg_proc.prosrc`). If the guard is missing, the gateway refuses to start in production — this prevents a migration rollback from silently removing the bypass and breaking erasure.

**Compliance profile enforcement gate.** Each tenant carries an optional `complianceProfile` field (set via `POST /v1/admin/tenants` or `PUT /v1/admin/tenants/{id}`) with the following allowed values: `none` (default), `soc2`, `fedramp`, `hipaa`. When `complianceProfile` is `soc2`, `fedramp`, or `hipaa`, the platform enforces SIEM as a hard requirement:

> **FedRAMP baseline note:** `complianceProfile: fedramp` activates the platform controls required for **FedRAMP Moderate** (AU-9 independent audit copy via SIEM, AU-11 1-year retention, AC-3 INSERT-only grants with periodic drift detection, and AU-12 pgaudit DDL/ROLE event capture). FedRAMP Moderate is the assumed baseline because it covers the majority of cloud SaaS use-cases and its 1-year AU-11 requirement is already the platform default. Deployers pursuing **FedRAMP High** must additionally set `audit.retentionPreset: fedramp-high` (3-year retention, see Section 16.4) and are responsible for the additional High-baseline controls that fall outside the platform's scope (e.g., FIPS 140-2 cryptographic modules, SC-28 encryption at rest for classified data, and any agency-specific control enhancements). **FedRAMP Low** is fully covered by `complianceProfile: fedramp` — Low's AU-11 requirement (1 year) and AU-9 requirements are a subset of Moderate. The platform does not claim FedRAMP authorization; `complianceProfile: fedramp` enables the relevant technical controls to support a deployer's own ATO process.

- **Tenant creation / update is rejected** with `COMPLIANCE_SIEM_REQUIRED` (HTTP 422) when `complianceProfile` is a regulated value and `audit.siem.endpoint` is not configured at the platform level. The error body includes: `"tenant.complianceProfile '<value>' requires audit.siem.endpoint to be configured. A database superuser can bypass INSERT-only grants; an independent SIEM copy is mandatory for compliance-grade audit integrity."` Deployers cannot bypass this gate — the only path forward is to configure the SIEM endpoint before creating or enabling a regulated tenant.
- **Environment creation within a regulated tenant is rejected** with the same error if `audit.siem.endpoint` becomes absent (e.g., removed from config) after the tenant was created. The gateway checks SIEM availability on every environment creation request when the parent tenant has a regulated `complianceProfile`.
- **Gateway startup with a regulated tenant and no SIEM is a fatal error** in production mode (`LENNY_ENV=production`). If any active tenant has `complianceProfile` in `{soc2, fedramp, hipaa}` and `audit.siem.endpoint` is absent, the gateway refuses to start with: `"FATAL: one or more tenants have a regulated complianceProfile but audit.siem.endpoint is not configured. Configure SIEM or downgrade tenant complianceProfile to 'none'."` This prevents a misconfiguration (e.g., SIEM endpoint accidentally removed from Helm values) from silently invalidating a live compliance posture.

**Audit integrity gap — Postgres-only deployments:** When `audit.siem.endpoint` is not configured and no tenant has a regulated `complianceProfile`, audit logs are stored exclusively in Postgres. In this configuration, the hash-chain mechanism (item 3) provides tamper-detection after the fact, but a database superuser who also has access to the `genesis_nonce` in the tenant configuration record can modify or delete rows and reconstruct a valid hash chain — the external SIEM is the only copy a superuser cannot alter. This means:

- **Tenants with a regulated `complianceProfile`** (soc2, fedramp, hipaa) **require** `audit.siem.endpoint` — enforced at tenant creation and gateway startup (see above). The SIEM provides the independent, immutable audit copy that satisfies regulatory requirements for separation of control from the audited system.
- **Development and single-tenant low-risk deployments** may operate without a SIEM, accepting this integrity limitation. This is appropriate for non-regulated workloads where a database superuser compromise is within the deployer's accepted risk profile.
- **Multi-tenant deployments with `complianceProfile: none`** are explicitly operating below compliance-grade integrity, regardless of tenant count. This must be a deliberate deployer decision (expressed by leaving `complianceProfile` at its default `none`), not an accidental configuration omission.

**Startup behavior when SIEM is unconfigured:**

- In **production mode** (`LENNY_ENV=production`) with any tenant whose `complianceProfile` is regulated (`soc2`, `fedramp`, `hipaa`): the gateway **refuses to start** with a fatal error (see enforcement gate above).
- In **production mode** with **multi-tenant configuration** (`tenancy.mode: multi`) and all tenants having `complianceProfile: none`: the gateway logs a `WARN`-level message at startup and every 60 seconds while running: `"WARNING: audit.siem.endpoint is not configured — audit logs are stored in Postgres only. A database superuser can bypass INSERT-only grants. This deployment does not meet compliance-grade audit integrity requirements (SOC2 CC7.2, FedRAMP AU-9, HIPAA §164.312(b)). Configure audit.siem.endpoint to resolve."` The `AuditSIEMNotConfigured` alert (Section 16.5) fires immediately. The gateway **does not refuse to start** — SIEM is not mandatory for unregulated deployments — but the integrity gap is made visible and persistent.
- In **production mode** with **single-tenant configuration** (`tenancy.mode: single`) and `complianceProfile: none`: the same `WARN` message is logged once at startup, without the repeating 60-second reminder. The `AuditSIEMNotConfigured` alert fires at `Warning` severity.
- In **non-production environments**: no warning is emitted. SIEM configuration is not expected outside production.

**Compliance-grade requirement summary:**

| `complianceProfile`       | SIEM configured? | Integrity Level                                                   | Compliance-Grade? | Allowed?                         |
| ------------------------- | ---------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------- | -------------------------------- |
| `soc2` / `fedramp` / `hipaa` | Yes           | Independent external copy; superuser bypass detectable            | Yes               | Yes                              |
| `soc2` / `fedramp` / `hipaa` | No            | —                                                                 | No                | **No — blocked at creation/startup** |
| `none`                    | Yes              | Independent external copy; superuser bypass detectable            | Yes               | Yes                              |
| `none`                    | No               | Hash-chain tamper-detection only; superuser can reconstruct chain | No                | Yes (with persistent warning)    |

**Superuser bypass mitigation:** Database superusers can bypass INSERT-only grants. The hash chaining mechanism (item 3) provides a detection layer — any superuser modification that alters or deletes rows is detectable by chain verification, provided an independent copy exists to compare against. The external SIEM stream (item 4) is that independent copy — a database superuser cannot modify the SIEM. When no SIEM is configured, hash-chain verification remains useful for detecting accidental corruption but provides weaker guarantees against a determined insider threat. Deployers operating under strict compliance requirements **should** configure `audit.siem.endpoint` and **should** additionally restrict superuser access via connection-level controls (e.g., separate superuser credentials stored in a hardware security module, accessed only through a privileged access management workflow).

### 11.8 Security Incident Response

The platform surfaces security signals and provides first-responder primitives; incident response procedures are the deployer's responsibility.

**Platform-surfaced security signals:**

| Signal | Section | Severity |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `AuditGrantDrift` — unexpected UPDATE/DELETE grants on audit tables | §11.7, §16.5 | Critical |
| `CredentialCompromised` — revoked credential still has active leases | §4.9, §16.5 | Critical |
| `DataResidencyViolationAttempt` — cross-region write or delegation bypass | §12.8, §16.5 | Critical |
| `AuditChainGap` — broken hash chain detected at startup | §11.7, §16.5 | Warning |
| `AuditSIEMNotConfigured` — no external audit copy in production | §11.7, §16.5 | Warning |

**Platform first-responder primitives:**

- **Credential revocation:** `POST /v1/admin/credential-pools/{id}/credentials/{cred_id}/revoke` immediately marks the credential as `revoked` and triggers active-lease propagation. The `CredentialCompromised` alert clears once all active leases are terminated. See Section 4.9.
- **User invalidation:** `POST /v1/admin/users/{user_id}/invalidate` terminates all active sessions for a user and revokes their tokens, stopping an active attacker's sessions. See Section 11.4.
- **Legal hold:** `POST /v1/admin/legal-hold` suspends retention rotation for any session or artifact implicated in an incident, preserving forensic state. See Section 12.8.
- **Audit trail:** All admin operations (revocations, holds, invalidations) are written to the append-only audit trail (Section 11.7) with operator identity and timestamp, providing a tamper-evident record of responder actions.

**Breach notification.** Lenny operates as a data processor. Breach notification obligations (GDPR Article 33/34, HIPAA §164.410, state data breach laws) are the deployer's responsibility as data controller. The platform provides the audit trail, SIEM stream, and erasure primitives deployers need to assess scope and demonstrate response, but it does not implement a notification workflow. Deployers must document their breach notification procedures in their incident response plans and ensure those procedures reference the platform signals listed above.

**Incident response plan guidance.** Deployers should define procedures covering at minimum: (1) triage criteria using the Critical alerts above; (2) first-responder steps using the primitives above; (3) scope assessment using the audit trail and SIEM; (4) breach notification timelines required by applicable law; (5) post-incident review and credential rotation. The platform's runbooks (`docs/runbooks/`) cover operational failures; security incident procedures are maintained separately by the deployer.

---

## 12. Storage Architecture

### 12.1 Design Principle

Abstract by **storage role**, not by raw database API. Each store exposes domain operations, not generic CRUD.

### 12.2 Storage Roles

| Role                  | Backend                                   | Purpose                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `SessionStore`        | Postgres                                  | Sessions, tasks, delegation tree, lineage, retry state                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| `LeaseStore`          | Redis (fallback: Postgres advisory locks) | Distributed session coordination                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| `TokenStore`          | Postgres (encrypted)                      | Downstream OAuth tokens, refresh tokens                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| `QuotaStore`          | Redis + Postgres                          | Rate limit counters, budget tracking                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| `ArtifactStore`       | MinIO (dev: local disk)                   | Uploaded files, checkpoints, workspace snapshots                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| `EventStore`          | Postgres                                  | Audit events, session logs, stream cursors                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| `CredentialPoolStore` | Postgres (encrypted)                      | Credential pool definitions, lease assignments, health scores, cooldown state, revocation state and deny-list references. Tenant-scoped: all tables carry `tenant_id` with RLS policies identical to other tenant-scoped stores (Section 4.2) |
| `EvictionStateStore`  | Postgres                                  | Minimal session state records written during eviction checkpoint fallback (conversation cursor, last message context)                                                                                                                         |
| `MemoryStore`         | Postgres (or pluggable)                   | User-scoped memories persisted across sessions (Section 9.4). Pluggable interface; deployers may substitute Mem0, Zep, or another vector database                            |
| `EvalResultStore`     | Postgres                                  | Eval scores and metadata keyed by `session_id`. FK dependency on `SessionStore` (`EvalResult.session_id -> sessions.id`)                                                      |
| `SemanticCache`       | Redis (or pluggable)                      | Cached LLM query/response pairs scoped by tenant, user, or session (Section 4.9). Disabled by default, opt-in per credential pool                                            |

All Redis-backed roles (`LeaseStore`, `QuotaStore`, `SemanticCache`, routing cache, token cache) **must** use the `t:{tenant_id}:` key prefix convention defined in Section 12.4 to enforce tenant isolation at the key-naming level.

The `ArtifactStore` (MinIO) **must** use `/{tenant_id}/` path prefixes for all object keys, with mandatory prefix validation at the interface level (see Sections 4.5 and 12.5).

### 12.3 Postgres HA Requirements

**Minimum topology:** Lenny requires a PostgreSQL instance (14+) with synchronous replication and automatic failover. This can be provided by a managed service (AWS RDS Multi-AZ, GCP Cloud SQL HA, Azure Database for PostgreSQL) or a self-managed operator (CloudNativePG, Patroni). Managed services are **recommended** as the default for most deployments. Per-tier Postgres instance sizing is in Section 17.8.

| Deployment             | Recommendation                                             |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cloud (production)     | Managed PostgreSQL with multi-AZ HA (e.g., RDS, Cloud SQL) |
| On-prem / self-managed | CloudNativePG operator or Patroni on Kubernetes            |
| Local dev              | Single PostgreSQL container (via docker-compose)           |

**Connection pooling:** A connection pooler is **required** in front of Postgres. Each gateway replica maintains a connection pool; without pooling, HPA-scaled replicas exhaust Postgres connection limits. **Cloud-managed deployments** should use the provider's built-in connection proxy (AWS RDS Proxy, GCP Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, Azure PgBouncer integration) instead of deploying a self-managed PgBouncer — see Section 17.9 for deployment profile guidance. **Self-managed deployments** deploy PgBouncer (or pgcat) as described below. Regardless of implementation, the pooler must operate in **transaction-mode** pooling (not session-mode) to ensure compatibility with RLS enforcement via `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant` (see Section 4.2) — `SET LOCAL` is transaction-scoped (cleared on `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`), so transaction-mode guarantees the setting does not leak across tenants sharing the same pooled connection. The pooler's `connect_query` (or equivalent initialization hook) must set a sentinel value (`SET app.current_tenant = '__unset__'`) on every fresh connection checkout so that any query reaching RLS without a prior `SET LOCAL` is rejected.

- **Deployment topology:** PgBouncer runs as a separate Deployment (minimum 2 replicas) fronted by a ClusterIP Service — not as a sidecar on each gateway pod. This centralizes pool management and avoids per-pod connection sprawl toward Postgres.
- **Pool mode:** Transaction mode (`pool_mode = transaction`). This is required for RLS enforcement because `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant` is transaction-scoped (cleared on `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK`); session mode would leak tenant context across unrelated requests sharing a backend connection.
- **Sizing guidance:** Set `default_pool_size` to approximately `max_connections / number_of_PgBouncer_replicas`, leaving headroom for superuser and replication connections. Configure `reserve_pool_size` (e.g., 5–10 per pool) for burst headroom, with `reserve_pool_timeout` set to a short duration (e.g., 3s) so reserved connections are only used under genuine load spikes.
- **HA:** PgBouncer is stateless — multiple replicas behind the Kubernetes ClusterIP Service provide transparent failover. If one replica is terminated or fails a health check, the Service routes traffic to surviving replicas with no client-side changes required.
- **PodDisruptionBudget:** A PDB **must** be configured for the PgBouncer Deployment with `minAvailable: 1` (or `maxUnavailable: 1` when running 3+ replicas). This prevents a rolling update or node drain from terminating all PgBouncer replicas simultaneously. Without the PDB, a bad rollout can take every replica through `CrashLoopBackOff` at once, making Postgres unreachable for all gateway traffic.
- **Readiness probe:** Each PgBouncer pod **must** define a readiness probe that verifies backend Postgres connectivity — not just PgBouncer process liveness. The probe should execute a lightweight query (e.g., `SELECT 1`) through PgBouncer's admin or application port to confirm that at least one backend connection can be established. A pod that starts successfully but cannot reach Postgres must be removed from the Service endpoints so traffic is routed only to replicas with verified backend connectivity. Recommended settings: `periodSeconds: 5`, `failureThreshold: 2`, `timeoutSeconds: 3`.
- **Full PgBouncer unavailability:** If all PgBouncer replicas are simultaneously unreachable (despite the PDB), gateway replicas lose access to Postgres. In this state the system behaves identically to a Postgres outage: Redis remains the primary coordination mechanism and all Redis-backed roles continue normally; new sessions and Postgres-dependent writes (including billing events — Section 11.2.1) are rejected with `503 Service Unavailable`. If Redis is also unavailable during this window, dual-store unavailability rules apply (Section 10.1). Recovery is automatic once at least one PgBouncer replica passes its readiness probe and rejoins the Service.
- **Monitoring:** Deploy `pgbouncer_exporter` as a sidecar on each PgBouncer pod to expose Prometheus metrics. Key metrics to alert on: pool utilization (`cl_active` / `sv_active` vs. pool size), client wait time (`cl_waiting_time`), and average query duration (`avg_query_time`).

**Cloud-managed pooler limitation — `__unset__` sentinel:** Most cloud-managed connection proxies (AWS RDS Proxy, GCP Cloud SQL Auth Proxy, Azure PgBouncer integration) do **not** support a `connect_query` or equivalent initialization hook. This means the `__unset__` sentinel — which is the second layer of RLS defense ensuring that any query reaching Postgres without a prior `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant` is rejected — cannot be set on connection checkout. Without the sentinel, a bug in the application-layer tenant context logic could silently allow cross-tenant reads, since only the application-layer `WHERE` clauses would remain as the sole defense.

**Alternative RLS defense for cloud-managed poolers:** Deployments using a cloud-managed connection proxy that lacks `connect_query` support **must** implement the following alternative defense:

1. **Per-transaction tenant validation trigger:** Create a Postgres `BEFORE` trigger (or RLS policy with a validation function) on every tenant-scoped table that verifies `current_setting('app.current_tenant', true)` is set and is not `NULL`, empty, or `'__unset__'` before allowing any `SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`. The trigger explicitly allows `'__all__'` (the `platform-admin` cross-tenant sentinel — see Section 4.2) in addition to concrete tenant IDs matching the `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,128}$` format. The full validation logic is: reject if value is `NULL`, empty string, or `'__unset__'`; allow if value is `'__all__'` or matches the tenant ID format. If the setting is missing or invalid, the trigger raises an exception (`ERRCODE 'P0001'`), blocking the query. This provides equivalent protection to the `connect_query` sentinel — any connection that reaches a tenant-scoped table without a prior `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant` in the current transaction will be rejected at the database level.
2. **Migration-enforced:** The trigger/function **must** be created by the Lenny schema migration (Section 12.3), not left to manual operator action. The migration detects whether `connect_query` sentinel support is available (via the `postgres.connectionPooler` Helm value) and creates the trigger when `connectionPooler = external`.
3. **`connectionPooler` defaults to `external` when `deploymentProfile = cloud-managed`:** When the `deploymentProfile` Helm value is `cloud-managed`, `postgres.connectionPooler` defaults to `external` if not explicitly set. This ensures cloud-managed deployments never silently operate without the `lenny_tenant_guard` trigger. Operators who wish to override this (e.g., because they have deployed a self-managed PgBouncer alongside a cloud database) must explicitly set `connectionPooler: pgbouncer`.
4. **Startup refusal without flag under cloud-managed poolers:** On gateway startup, after Postgres connectivity is confirmed, the gateway detects the active pooler mode by inspecting the `LENNY_POOLER_MODE` environment variable (injected by the Helm chart from `postgres.connectionPooler`). If `LENNY_POOLER_MODE = external` and the `lenny_tenant_guard` trigger does not exist on tenant-scoped tables (verified by querying `pg_trigger`), the gateway **refuses to start** and exits with a fatal error: `"FATAL: cloud-managed pooler mode (LENNY_POOLER_MODE=external) detected but lenny_tenant_guard trigger is absent from tenant-scoped tables — RLS tenant isolation defense is not active; run schema migrations before starting the gateway (Section 12.3)"`. This check is independent of the preflight Job so that it also catches trigger removal after initial installation (e.g., manual migration rollback).
5. **Integration test requirement:** An integration test (`TestRLSTenantGuardMissingSetLocal`) **must** exist that: (a) connects to a test Postgres instance with the `lenny_tenant_guard` trigger deployed; (b) opens a transaction without issuing `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant`; (c) executes a `SELECT` against a tenant-scoped table; and (d) asserts that the query raises an exception (verifying the trigger blocks the access). A second case in the same test must open a transaction for tenant A and attempt to read rows belonging to tenant B (by setting `SET LOCAL app.current_tenant = 'tenant-a'` then querying rows seeded under `'tenant-b'`) and assert zero rows are returned. This test must run in the CI pipeline on every pull request.
6. **Performance note:** The per-transaction trigger adds negligible overhead (a single `current_setting()` call per statement), but operators should monitor `pg_stat_user_functions` to verify trigger execution counts align with query volume.

See Section 17.8 for per-tier Postgres and PgBouncer sizing recommendations.

**Write IOPS estimation:** Multiple gateway components generate Postgres writes concurrently: session state updates, quota checkpoint flushes, billing event inserts, and audit log inserts. The following table estimates sustained write IOPS at each capacity tier (see Section 16.5 for tier definitions, Section 17.8 for instance sizing):

| Write Source                   | Tier 1    | Tier 2     | Tier 3       | Notes                                                  |
| ------------------------------ | --------- | ---------- | ------------ | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Session state updates          | ~5/s      | ~50/s      | ~300/s       | Per-session lifecycle transitions, heartbeats          |
| Quota checkpoint flushes       | ~2/s      | ~20/s      | ~100/s       | Redis→Postgres periodic sync (Section 10.1)            |
| Billing event inserts          | ~10/s     | ~100/s     | ~600/s       | Per-token and per-session events (Section 11.2.1)      |
| Audit log inserts              | ~5/s      | ~50/s      | ~300/s       | Per-request and lifecycle audit entries (Section 11.7) |
| **Total estimated write IOPS** | **~22/s** | **~220/s** | **~1,300/s** | Sustained; bursts may reach 2–3× during session storms |

At Tier 3, total sustained write IOPS (~1,300/s) with burst headroom (~3,900/s) approaches the practical write ceiling of a single 8-vCPU Postgres primary with synchronous replication. Operators should monitor `pg_stat_bgwriter` (buffers written), `pg_stat_wal` (WAL write rate), and replication lag to detect write pressure before it impacts latency.

**Batching guidance for write-heavy paths:** Billing event inserts and audit log inserts are the two highest-volume write sources and are append-only (no UPDATE or DELETE). Billing events **should** be batched to reduce per-statement overhead and WAL amplification. Audit log write mode is governed by data classification — T3/T4 audit events are always synchronous; T2 batching is opt-in only (see below):

- **Billing events:** The gateway **should** buffer billing events in memory and flush them as multi-row `INSERT` statements at a configurable interval (`billingFlushIntervalMs`, default: 500ms) or when the buffer reaches a size threshold (`billingFlushBatchSize`, default: 50 events). On graceful shutdown, the gateway flushes any remaining buffered events before exiting. If the buffer exceeds `billingFlushMaxPending` (default: 500), the gateway flushes immediately and emits the `billing_flush_pressure` metric.
- **Audit logs:** Audit log write mode is determined by **data classification tier** (Section 12.9), not by environment or SIEM configuration alone.

  **Default: synchronous writes for T3/T4 audit events (unconditional).** User-scoped audit events (T3 — Confidential; includes user identifiers and user-scoped records per Section 12.9) and any T4 — Restricted audit events are written **synchronously by default** with no batching buffer. `auditFlushIntervalMs` is ignored for these events regardless of `LENNY_ENV` or SIEM configuration. The synchronous write path does not block request processing — audit writes use a separate goroutine with a dedicated connection pool (`audit.syncWritePoolSize`, default: 4 connections).

  **T2 (non-PII) audit events — batching is an explicit opt-in.** Non-PII audit events (T2 — Internal; e.g., pool metrics, routing events) may use batched inserts via `auditFlushIntervalMs` (default: **250ms**; `auditFlushBatchSize`, default: 100 entries). **Batching must be explicitly enabled** by setting `audit.batchingEnabled: true` in the Helm configuration. This option is disabled by default. When enabled, the deployment documentation must record the accepted data-loss risk.

  > **WARNING: `audit.batchingEnabled: true` accepts data loss.** The audit flush buffer is in-memory and has no write-ahead log. A gateway replica crash during a flush interval will silently lose buffered T2 audit events not yet written to Postgres. Estimated loss at each tier:
  >
  > | Tier   | Audit write rate | Max events lost per crash (250ms window) |
  > | ------ | ---------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
  > | Tier 1 | ~5/s             | ~2 events                                |
  > | Tier 2 | ~50/s            | ~13 events                               |
  > | Tier 3 | ~300/s           | ~75 events                               |
  >
  > T3 and T4 audit events are **never** batched regardless of this setting.

  **When SIEM is configured — outbox pattern (Postgres-first):** Audit events are **always written to Postgres synchronously first**, regardless of SIEM configuration. SIEM forwarding happens asynchronously via an outbox/change-data-capture pattern: a dedicated forwarder process (or logical-replication consumer) tails the audit table and delivers events to the SIEM after they are durably committed to Postgres. This design ensures a gateway crash before SIEM delivery cannot lose events — the records are already committed in Postgres and will be forwarded by the outbox forwarder on recovery. The SIEM is an independent immutable copy, not the primary durable store.

  Consequently, **SIEM configuration does not relax the T2 batching restriction.** Even with a SIEM endpoint configured, T2 audit event batching remains an explicit opt-in (`audit.batchingEnabled: true`) that accepts data loss for the in-memory buffer window. The rationale: the outbox forwarder reads committed Postgres rows — any T2 event not yet flushed from the in-memory buffer to Postgres before a crash is lost from both Postgres and the SIEM. HIPAA AU-9, FedRAMP AU-10, and SOC2 CC7.2 require completeness; batching is only appropriate when the deployer has accepted the documented data-loss risk for non-PII T2 events.

  **Outbox forwarder requirements:** The forwarder must use Postgres logical replication (`wal_level = logical`) or poll the audit table using the `sequence` column as a high-water mark. It must checkpoint its delivery position durably (e.g., a `siem_delivery_state` table) so that a forwarder restart replays from the last confirmed delivery point without duplication or gap. Delivery acknowledgement from the SIEM must be recorded before the high-water mark is advanced. Operators must monitor `siem_delivery_lag_seconds` (the gap between the latest committed audit event and the latest SIEM-acknowledged event); the `AuditSIEMDeliveryLag` alert (Section 16.5) fires when lag exceeds the configured threshold (`audit.siem.maxDeliveryLagSeconds`, default: 30s).

  **When no SIEM is configured (Postgres-only deployments):** T3/T4 audit events remain synchronous (as above). For T2 events, `audit.batchingEnabled: true` may be used but the data-loss warning above applies unconditionally — there is no external durable copy to recover from on crash. In `LENNY_ENV=production` mode without SIEM, the gateway logs `"Audit batching is enabled for T2 events but no SIEM is configured — buffered T2 audit events will be lost on gateway crash"` at startup and emits the `AuditBatchingNoSIEM` metric counter. In non-production environments, batching for T2 events is permitted without warning when `audit.batchingEnabled: true`.

  **Startup chain-continuity check:** On gateway startup, after Postgres becomes reachable, the gateway verifies that the audit hash chain is unbroken for each active tenant by querying the most recent N entries (configurable via `audit.startupChainCheckEntries`, default: 1000) and re-verifying the `prev_hash` chain. If a gap is detected (broken chain), the gateway emits a `WARN`-level log message identifying the tenant, the sequence numbers of the gap boundaries, and the approximate timestamp range of the missing entries: `"Audit chain gap detected for tenant <id>: gap between sequence <n> and <m> (~<timestamp range>). This indicates T2 audit events were lost from the in-memory batch buffer during a previous gateway crash. T3/T4 events are synchronous and will not appear in chain gaps."` The `AuditChainGap` alert (Section 16.5) fires. The gateway does **not** refuse to start — a gap is a compliance signal, not a fatal error. Operators are responsible for documenting the gap in their compliance records. Because T2 events were never committed to Postgres before the crash, neither the SIEM (which is fed by the Postgres outbox) nor Postgres will have a copy of the lost events; the gap cannot be replayed.

- **Separate Postgres for write-heavy paths (Tier 3):** At Tier 3, operators **may** deploy a dedicated Postgres instance for billing and audit writes to isolate write amplification from the primary instance handling session state and quota checkpoints. The gateway supports separate connection strings for the billing/audit write path (`LENNY_PG_BILLING_AUDIT_DSN`). When configured, billing and audit inserts are routed to this instance while all other writes continue to the primary. This is optional — a single well-provisioned primary handles Tier 3 sustained load, but the separate instance provides headroom for burst absorption and reduces replication lag on the primary.

**Write classification and load shedding strategy (Tier 3):** Not all writes are equally critical. At Tier 3 sustained load (~1,300/s) with burst headroom (~3,900/s), the primary Postgres instance may become write-saturated. The following classification governs how write pressure is relieved before and during saturation:

| Write Source                      | Classification       | Load-Shedding Behaviour                                                                                                                                  |
| --------------------------------- | -------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Session state transitions         | **SLO-critical**     | Never deferred or dropped. Session correctness depends on these writes. If Postgres cannot accept the write, the session creation or transition is rejected with a retryable error. |
| Quota checkpoint flushes          | **SLO-critical**     | Never dropped; may be deferred by increasing `quotaSyncIntervalSeconds` up to 60s (maximum) under sustained write pressure. Redis counters remain accurate; Postgres is a durable checkpoint only. |
| Billing event inserts             | **Best-effort durable** | On Postgres unavailability, events are first staged to Redis stream (`t:{tenant_id}:billing:stream`, MAXLEN per `billingRedisStreamMaxLen` -- see Section 17.8.2 for per-tier defaults, TTL 3600s) and flushed to Postgres by background goroutine on recovery. If Redis is also unavailable, events fall back to bounded in-memory write-ahead buffer (default: 10,000 events). Buffer overflow rejects new session-progressing requests (503). NOT dropped unless Redis + gateway crash simultaneously. |
| Audit log inserts (T3/T4)         | **SLO-critical**     | Always synchronous; never batched or deferred. Write saturation on the primary Postgres instance is the trigger to activate the separate billing/audit instance (`LENNY_PG_BILLING_AUDIT_DSN`). |
| Audit log inserts (T2)            | **Best-effort**      | Batched (opt-in); may be lost on crash. Highest tolerable drop under write saturation — increasing `auditFlushIntervalMs` to 500ms reduces write rate at the cost of increased data-loss exposure. |

**Per-tier write ceiling reference table.** The `PostgresWriteSaturation` alert (Section 16.5) fires when sustained write IOPS exceed 80% of the estimated instance ceiling for more than 5 minutes. The following table defines the numeric ceilings used to evaluate that threshold. These values assume the recommended instance class for each tier (Section 17.8.2); operators who provision a smaller instance must scale the ceilings proportionally.

| Tier   | Recommended primary instance | Estimated write ceiling (sustained) | 80% alert threshold | Burst ceiling (~3× sustained) |
| ------ | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| Tier 1 | 2 vCPU / 4 GB (e.g., `db.t3.medium`) | ~200 IOPS | ~160 IOPS | ~600 IOPS |
| Tier 2 | 4 vCPU / 16 GB (e.g., `db.r6g.xlarge`) | ~600 IOPS | ~480 IOPS | ~1,800 IOPS |
| Tier 3 | 8 vCPU / 32 GB (e.g., `db.r6g.2xlarge`) | ~1,600 IOPS | ~1,280 IOPS | ~4,800 IOPS |

These ceilings are empirical estimates derived from Postgres WAL write benchmarks on the reference instance types, not theoretical maximums. Operators MUST validate them in Phase 2 load tests (Tier 1/2) and Phase 13.5 load tests (Tier 3) and update the Helm `postgres.writeCeilingIops` override if their workload or instance type differs. The `PostgresWriteSaturation` alert uses the configured ceiling value, not a hard-coded constant — set `postgres.writeCeilingIops` to the measured ceiling for accurate alerting.

> **Workload-specific write overhead not captured in the estimates above:** The generic WAL benchmark numbers do not account for two Lenny-specific write overhead sources that must be quantified by the Phase 13.5 Lenny-specific write-pattern benchmark (scenario 8): (1) **RLS trigger overhead** — the `lenny_tenant_guard` trigger (Section 12.3) executes on every tenant-scoped statement; under high write concurrency the per-call overhead (a `current_setting()` call + conditional abort check) can add measurable WAL amplification, particularly for the audit log and billing event paths where every row write is tenant-scoped. Monitor `pg_stat_user_functions` under load to quantify trigger execution time. (2) **Quota flush burst patterns** — the Redis→Postgres periodic quota checkpoint sync (Section 10.1) produces bursty writes: at the flush interval boundary, all accumulated quota deltas for active sessions are written using batched multi-row `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT UPDATE` statements (`quotaFlushBatchSize`, default: 200 rows per statement). To prevent all gateway replicas from flushing simultaneously, each replica adds per-replica jitter: `quotaSyncIntervalSeconds + random(0, quotaSyncIntervalSeconds × 0.3)`, spreading flush bursts across a ~3-second window at Tier 3. **Per-tier expected peak flush IOPS** (worst case, all sessions with quota changes): Tier 1: ~5 IOPS (negligible); Tier 2: ~50 IOPS; Tier 3: `ceil(10,000 / 200) = 50` batched statements per replica, with 30 replicas staggered across the jitter window, yielding ~500 peak IOPS within any 1-second measurement window. At Tier 3 the margin between estimated sustained load (~1,300/s) and the instance ceiling (~1,600/s) is approximately 18% (~300 IOPS headroom), which accommodates the staggered flush spike but leaves limited margin for coincident overhead. The `PostgresWriteBurstIops` alert (Section 16.5) fires when instantaneous write IOPS exceed the burst ceiling; repeated triggers indicate quota flush storms that require either increasing the flush interval or provisioning a larger instance class.

**Horizontal write scaling route:** A single Postgres primary has no horizontal write scale-out path. The defined scaling route is:

1. **Vertical scaling first:** Increase the primary instance class (Section 17.8). An 8-vCPU primary handles ~1,300/s sustained; a 16-vCPU instance extends the ceiling to approximately 2,500–3,000/s.
2. **Instance separation (Tier 3 trigger):** When primary write IOPS sustains above 80% of the instance ceiling, activate the separate billing/audit Postgres instance (`LENNY_PG_BILLING_AUDIT_DSN`). This offloads the two largest append-only write sources (~900/s combined at Tier 3), reducing primary write load to ~400/s (session state + quota checkpoints).
3. **Append-only table partitioning:** If the separated billing/audit instance is also approaching saturation, partition the `billing_events` and `audit_log` tables by time range (monthly or weekly partitions). Postgres declarative partitioning (`PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at)`) allows parallel writes to the current partition and fast detach/archive of old partitions without vacuuming the full table. This is the primary mitigation for sustained write growth beyond vertical scaling.
4. **Write-ahead buffer as back-pressure:** The `billingWriteAheadBufferSize` limit acts as back-pressure — the gateway stops accepting new billable work rather than silently dropping billing records. This is intentional: billing completeness is a hard invariant. Operators must monitor `billing_write_ahead_buffer_utilization` and alert before it reaches 80%.

Operators must monitor `pg_stat_bgwriter` (buffers_clean, maxwritten_clean), `pg_stat_wal` (wal_bytes/s), and replication lag. The `PostgresWriteSaturation` alert (Section 16.5) fires when sustained write IOPS exceed 80% of the estimated instance ceiling for more than 5 minutes.

**Shard-key index discipline (R-01).** Every tenant-scoped table MUST have `tenant_id` as the leading column of its primary index or as the first element of every composite index used on high-frequency query paths. This ensures that queries targeting a specific tenant land on the correct shard in a future sharded topology without full-table scans. **Session-sharded table exemption:** Tables routed by `StoreRouter.SessionShard(sessionID)` — i.e., `sessions`, `session_messages`, `session_tree_archive`, and related per-session tables — are exempt from the leading-`tenant_id` primary key requirement because their distribution key is the shard routing prefix embedded in the session ID (see §12.6 session ID format), not `tenant_id`. All sessions in a delegation tree share the same routing prefix and co-locate on the same shard, enabling single-shard tree traversal, cascade cancellation, and delegation lineage queries. These tables MUST still include a `(tenant_id, ...)` secondary index for scatter-gather queries (`GET /v1/sessions`, GDPR erasure, tenant deletion) and RLS filtering. The CI linter must accept either `tenant_id` or `id`/`session_id` as the leading primary key column for tables annotated `-- session-sharded`. **Phase 1.5 CI check requirement:** The CI pipeline MUST include a schema linter (`scripts/lint-schema.sh` or equivalent) that verifies no tenant-scoped table has an index whose leading column is not `tenant_id` (unless annotated `-- session-sharded`). The check must run on every pull request that modifies a migration file. Migrations that introduce non-conforming indexes are rejected at CI time, not discovered in production. **Annotation validation:** The `-- session-sharded` annotation MUST appear only on `CREATE TABLE` statements for tables that have a foreign key referencing `sessions(id)` either directly or transitively (e.g., `session_messages.session_id → sessions.id`, `session_tree_archive.root_session_id → sessions.id`). The linter MUST verify this FK relationship exists in the same migration or a prior committed migration — an annotation without a verifiable FK is a linter error. The set of session-sharded tables is intentionally small and closed: `sessions`, `session_messages`, `session_tree_archive`, and any future table whose primary access pattern is single-session lookup by embedded routing prefix. Adding a new `-- session-sharded` annotation MUST be accompanied by a `-- session-sharded-justification: <reason>` comment on the preceding line (mirroring the R-02 justification pattern for `-- platform-admin-cross-tenant-allowed`). The linter MUST report the total count of `-- session-sharded` annotations and fail if the count increases without a justification.

**No cross-tenant JOIN rule (R-02).** Application code MUST NOT issue SQL queries that JOIN across tenant boundaries — i.e., no query may `JOIN` a tenant-scoped table from one tenant against rows belonging to a different tenant. This rule exists independently of RLS enforcement: even though RLS filters rows at the database level, a cross-tenant JOIN in application code indicates a logic error that will surface as data leakage if RLS is misconfigured or bypassed. A static-analysis linter (`scripts/lint-queries.sh` or equivalent) MUST enforce this rule by detecting JOIN operations between tables that carry `tenant_id` columns where the `ON` clause does not include `a.tenant_id = b.tenant_id`. The linter must run in CI on every pull request. The only permitted exception is the `platform-admin` cross-tenant reporting path (Section 4.2), which must be explicitly annotated with `-- platform-admin-cross-tenant-allowed` in the query source to suppress the linter warning. Three additional safeguards apply to every annotated query:

1. **Pairing rule:** Every query annotated `-- platform-admin-cross-tenant-allowed` MUST be in a code path that (a) sets `app.current_tenant = '__all__'` via the platform-admin gateway path (Section 4.2), and (b) is unreachable from non-`platform-admin` callers. The linter SHOULD verify that annotated queries appear only in files under a `platform-admin`-designated package (e.g., `internal/admin/platform/`). The annotation alone is not sufficient — it must be paired with a code path that enforces `platform-admin` RBAC before reaching the query.
2. **Audit event:** Every code path that sets `app.current_tenant = '__all__'` MUST emit an audit event (`cross_tenant_read`) recording the caller identity, endpoint, and query category (e.g., `tenant_list`, `usage_dashboard`, `erasure_job`). This is one event per API call, not per SQL statement.
3. **Annotation inventory:** The CI linter MUST report the total count of `-- platform-admin-cross-tenant-allowed` annotations and fail if a new annotation is added without an accompanying `-- platform-admin-cross-tenant-justification: <reason>` comment on the preceding line. This ensures new cross-tenant query paths get explicit justification visible in code review.

**StoreRouter requirement for billing/audit writes (R-03).** All billing event inserts and audit log inserts MUST be routed through the `StoreRouter` interface (Section 12.6), even in v1 where `StoreRouter` resolves to a single `SingleShardRouter`. This rule prevents the billing and audit write paths from hardcoding a direct Postgres connection that bypasses shard routing. In v1, `StoreRouter.BillingShard(tenantID)` and `StoreRouter.AuditShard(tenantID)` return the sole configured Postgres pool, so there is no behavioral change; the discipline ensures that adding a second shard in a future release requires only a new `StoreRouter` implementation, not a scan for direct connection usage throughout the billing and audit paths. An integration test (`TestBillingAuditRoutedThroughStoreRouter`) MUST verify that billing event inserts and audit log inserts call `StoreRouter` methods rather than accessing a Postgres pool directly.

**Read replicas:** The gateway should use separate connection strings for read and write traffic. Read-heavy queries (session status, task tree, audit reads, usage reports) should be routed to read replicas. Most managed services provide a reader endpoint for this purpose. Write traffic goes to the primary only.

**RPO/RTO targets:**

- RPO: 0 (synchronous replication — no committed transaction lost)
- RTO: < 30s (automatic failover)

**Behavior during Postgres failover:** During the Postgres failover window (up to 30s), Redis remains the primary coordination mechanism and all Redis-backed roles continue normally. If Redis is also unavailable during this window, dual-store unavailability rules apply (see Section 10.1, "Dual-store unavailability"). Existing sessions with cached coordination state continue; new sessions and writes requiring Postgres (including billing events — see Section 11.2.1) are rejected or queued as specified below.

**Write durability categories during failover:**

| Write category                               | Durability during failover                                                                                  | Recovery mechanism                                                                                                                                      |
| -------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Committed session state transitions          | **Durable** — synchronous replication guarantees no committed transaction is lost (RPO = 0)                 | Automatic; new primary has all committed writes                                                                                                         |
| In-flight (uncommitted) session state writes | **At-risk** — transactions not yet committed at failover time are lost by definition                        | Gateway retries the state transition after reconnecting to the new primary; `coordination_generation` (Section 10.1) prevents stale or duplicate writes |
| Billing events in write-ahead buffer         | **Durable (Redis available) / At-risk (Redis also down)** — events are first staged to Redis stream (`t:{tenant_id}:billing:stream`) which survives individual gateway crashes; in-memory write-ahead buffer is only used when Redis is also unavailable | Redis stream flushed to Postgres by background goroutine on recovery. In-memory buffer events reconstructed from pod-reported token usage (Section 7.3). See maximum billing gap below. |
| Quota counters (Redis-primary)               | **Unaffected** — Redis continues independently of Postgres                                                  | Postgres checkpoint (every `quotaSyncIntervalSeconds`, default 30s) provides durable fallback                                                           |
| Coordination leases (Redis-primary)          | **Unaffected** — Redis continues independently of Postgres                                                  | Postgres advisory lock fallback if Redis also fails                                                                                                     |
| Audit events (T3/T4) in flush buffer         | **Durable** — synchronous writes by default; no in-memory buffer for T3/T4 events (Section 12.3)           | No recovery needed; synchronous writes are committed before the gateway processes the next request on the audit write path                              |
| Audit events (T2) in flush buffer            | **At-risk if batching enabled** — in-memory buffer lost on gateway crash when `audit.batchingEnabled: true` | T2 events not yet flushed to Postgres are permanently lost — the SIEM outbox forwarder reads from Postgres, so events absent from Postgres are absent from SIEM too (Section 12.3). Batching is opt-in and disabled by default. |

**Maximum billing gap:** With the Redis stream intermediate buffer, the billing gap scenario is significantly narrowed. The worst-case permanent billing gap now requires: a gateway replica crashes **simultaneously** with both a Postgres failover **and** Redis unavailability, while its in-memory write-ahead buffer (Tier 2 fallback) is non-empty and the pods it coordinated also fail (preventing reconstruction from pod-reported usage). This triple-failure scenario (gateway crash + Postgres failover + Redis outage + pod loss) is the only path to permanent billing data loss. Under the common failure case (Postgres failover with Redis available), all billing events are durably staged to the Redis stream and flushed to Postgres on recovery — zero billing loss. Under the Redis stream path, bounded gap per replica is: `billingFlushIntervalMs` (default 500ms) worth of events that were in-flight between `XADD` to Redis and `XDEL` after Postgres confirmation at the moment of the simultaneous crash — at most ~30 events (60 events/s × 500ms). Under the in-memory-only fallback path (Redis also unavailable), the bounded gap is still ~30 events for the same reasons. If coordinated pods survive (the common case), pod-reported cumulative totals allow full reconstruction with zero billing loss regardless of which buffer tier was active.

**Encryption at rest:** Postgres storage must be encrypted at rest. Managed services (RDS, Cloud SQL, Azure Database) provide this by default via volume-level encryption — verify it is enabled. Self-managed deployments must use LUKS-encrypted volumes or dm-crypt for the Postgres data directory. All WAL archives and base backups must also be encrypted: managed services encrypt backups automatically when storage encryption is enabled; self-managed deployments must use server-side encryption on the backup target (e.g., SSE-S3/SSE-KMS for MinIO/S3 backup destinations) or client-side encryption (e.g., `gpg` or age) before upload.

**Backups:** Daily base backups + continuous WAL archival. Restore tested monthly (see Section 17.3 for the `lenny-restore-test` CronJob procedure). All backup and WAL archive storage must be encrypted at rest as specified above.

### 12.4 Redis HA and Failure Modes

**Minimum topology:** For self-managed deployments: Redis Sentinel (3 sentinels, 1 primary + 1 replica). Redis Cluster if sharding is needed at Tier 3. For cloud-managed deployments: use the provider's managed cache service (AWS ElastiCache, GCP Memorystore, Azure Cache for Redis) which provides equivalent HA, replication, and scaling — see Section 17.9 for deployment profile guidance. See Section 17.8 for per-tier Redis topology recommendations.

**Tenant key isolation:** All Redis keys **must** use the prefix `t:{tenant_id}:` followed by the storage role and logical key. The following table lists all canonical key prefix patterns in use:

| Key prefix pattern | Role | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `t:{tenant_id}:lease:session:{session_id}` | Session coordination lease | `SET NX` with TTL; falls back to Postgres on miss |
| `t:{tenant_id}:quota:tokens:{user_id}:{window}` | Per-user token quota counter | `INCR`; reconciled with Postgres on recovery |
| `t:{tenant_id}:session:{session_id}:dlq` | Dead-letter queue for session messages | Sorted set scored by expiry; see §7.2 |
| `t:{tenant_id}:billing:stream` | Billing write-ahead stream | Redis stream, MAXLEN per `billingRedisStreamMaxLen` (50,000 at Tier 1/2, 72,000 at Tier 3 -- see §17.8.2), TTL 3600s; flushed to Postgres on recovery; purged on user erasure (see §12.8) |
| `t:{tenant_id}:exp:{experiment_id}:sticky:{user_id}` | Experiment sticky assignment cache | Flushed on experiment pause/conclude; see §10.7 |
| `t:{tenant_id}:session:{session_id}:inbox` | Durable session inbox (Redis list) | Created when `messaging.durableInbox: true`; enqueue/dequeue/recovery via `LPUSH`/`LREM`/`LRANGE`; see §7.2 |
| `t:{tenant_id}:scache:{scope}:{hash}` | Semantic cache entry | `scope` is `u:{user_id}`, `s:{session_id}`, or `t` per `cacheScope`; `hash` is the similarity lookup key. See §4.9 (Semantic Caching) |
| `t:{tenant_id}:evt:{topic}` | EventBus pub/sub channel | **Tenant-scoped.** `{topic}` is one of the `EventTopic` constants (`delegation_tree`, `session_lifecycle`). Channel name is constructed by the `EventBus` implementation — callers never build raw channel names. See §12.6 EventBus. |
| `lenny:pod:{pod_id}:active_slots` | Concurrent-workspace slot counter | **Pod-scoped** (not tenant-prefixed — intentional, as pod IDs are already globally unique within the cluster). Tracks the number of concurrently active slots on a given pod. On Redis restart, counters reset to zero; the gateway performs **blocking rehydration** from `SessionStore.GetActiveSlotsByPod(pod_id)` before accepting new slot assignments — see §5.2 ("Post-recovery rehydration atomicity") for the full protocol. The Lua script checks a per-pod `rehydrated` flag (`lenny:pod:{pod_id}:rehydrated`) atomically; if absent, all slot allocation attempts for that pod block until rehydration completes, preventing concurrent requests from observing a stale zero counter. |
| `lenny:pod:{pod_id}:rehydrated` | Slot counter rehydration flag | **Pod-scoped.** Set to `1` after the gateway rehydrates `active_slots` from Postgres post-Redis-restart. No TTL — naturally cleared on next Redis restart. Checked atomically by the slot assignment Lua script; absence triggers blocking rehydration. See §5.2. |
| `lenny:pod:{pod_id}:rehydrating` | Rehydration coordination lock | **Pod-scoped.** Short-lived `SET NX` lock used to coordinate cross-replica rehydration so only one replica queries Postgres per pod. TTL: `slotRehydrationTimeoutMs` (default 2000ms). See §5.2. |
| `cb:{name}` | Circuit breaker state | **Platform-scoped** (not tenant-prefixed -- circuit breakers are platform-wide operational controls, not tenant-scoped data). Value: `{state, reason, opened_at, opened_by}`. See §11.6. |
