| `{root_session_id}:dlg:tokens`, `{root_session_id}:dlg:tree_size`, `{root_session_id}:dlg:tree_memory`, `{root_session_id}:dlg:parallel_children:{parent_session_id}`, `{root_session_id}:dlg:children_total:{parent_session_id}` | Delegation budget counters | **Tree-scoped** (not tenant-prefixed — intentional exception to the `t:{tenant_id}:` rule; see §8.3 R-04). The `{root_session_id}` hash tag distributes different delegation trees across Redis Cluster slots while co-locating all keys for one tree on the same slot (required for multi-key Lua atomicity in `budget_reserve.lua` / `budget_return.lua`). Using `{tenant_id}` would create a hot slot for tenants with many concurrent trees. Tenant isolation is enforced at the gateway application layer: the gateway validates that `root_session_id` belongs to the calling tenant (via `SessionStore` under RLS) before invoking any delegation budget Lua script. Tree-wide keys (`dlg:tokens`, `dlg:tree_size`, `dlg:tree_memory`) are shared across the entire delegation tree. Per-parent keys (`dlg:parallel_children:{parent_session_id}`, `dlg:children_total:{parent_session_id}`) are scoped to each delegating parent within the tree, enabling per-node `maxParallelChildren` and `maxChildrenTotal` enforcement. All keys share the same `{root_session_id}` hash tag, preserving single-slot co-location. |

DLQ keys and billing stream keys follow the same tenant prefix convention. The `lenny:pod:{pod_id}:*` keys (`active_slots`, `rehydrated`, `rehydrating`), `cb:{name}` circuit breaker keys, and `{root_session_id}:dlg:*` delegation budget keys are the three exception classes to the tenant-prefix rule — pod IDs are cluster-globally unique and pod-scoped, circuit breaker keys are platform-wide operational controls, and delegation budget keys use a tree-scoped hash tag for Redis Cluster slot distribution (see §8.3 R-04 for rationale). This convention is enforced in the Redis wrapper layer; no raw Redis command may be issued without the tenant prefix (or pod prefix for slot counters, or `cb:` prefix for circuit breakers, or `{root_session_id}:dlg:` prefix for delegation budget keys — the wrapper validates the calling tenant owns the `root_session_id` before permitting the operation). An integration test (`TestRedisTenantKeyIsolation`) must verify that operations scoped to one tenant cannot read or mutate keys belonging to another tenant. The test suite **must include DLQ, inbox, semantic cache, and delegation budget key coverage**: (a) a DLQ write for tenant A must not be readable by a DLQ processor scoped to tenant B, and (b) a DLQ processor performing a cross-tenant `ZRANGEBYSCORE` scan must return zero results when the key prefix belongs to a different tenant, and (c) a durable inbox enqueue for tenant A's session must not be visible to a coordinator scoped to tenant B, and (d) a semantic cache write for tenant A must not produce a cache hit for a semantically identical query from tenant B, and (e) a delegation budget `budget_reserve.lua` invocation for a `root_session_id` owned by tenant A must be rejected when the gateway context is scoped to tenant B, and a `budget_return.lua` invocation for the same `root_session_id` must likewise be rejected — verifying the application-layer tenant ownership check that compensates for the absence of a `t:{tenant_id}:` key prefix on delegation budget keys (both scripts must be tested because they have distinct attack profiles: `budget_reserve` steals allocation, `budget_return` inflates available budget), and (f) an EventBus publish on tenant A's `t:{tenant_id}:evt:delegation_tree` channel must not be received by a subscriber scoped to tenant B's channel for the same topic.

**Security:** Redis AUTH (ACLs) and TLS are **required**. Cached access tokens are encrypted before storage in Redis (not stored as plaintext). Tokens are encrypted using AES-256-GCM with a key derived from the Token Service's envelope encryption key; each cached token is stored as `{nonce || ciphertext || tag}`, and the encryption key is rotated alongside the envelope key (Section 4.9.1).

**Data-at-rest posture:** Redis data is treated as **ephemeral** — every Redis-backed role (leases, quota counters, routing cache, token cache) has a durable fallback or reconstruction path (see failure behavior table below). Redis is not a system of record, and total data loss is recoverable. Nonetheless, sensitive cached values (access tokens, credential lease references) must be encrypted at the application layer before writing to Redis, as specified above. Volume-level encryption for Redis persistence files (RDB/AOF) is recommended for defense-in-depth but is not a substitute for app-layer encryption of sensitive fields.

**Failure behavior per use case:**

| Use Case                   | On Redis Unavailability                                                                                                      |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Rate limit counters        | **Fail open with bounded window** — allow requests for up to `rateLimitFailOpenMaxSeconds` (default: 60s), then fail closed  |
| Distributed session leases | **Fall back** to Postgres advisory locks (higher latency)                                                                    |
| Routing cache              | **Fall back** to Postgres lookup                                                                                             |
| Cached access tokens       | **Re-fetch** from TokenStore (Postgres)                                                                                      |
| Quota counters             | **Fail open with per-replica budget ceiling** — enforce conservative per-tenant limits locally, then fail closed (see below) |
| Storage quota counter (`storage_bytes_used`) | **Postgres fallback, then fail closed** — on Redis unavailability, the gateway rehydrates each tenant's `storage_bytes_used` counter from the sum of `artifact_size_bytes` across active artifacts in Postgres (same rehydration path as token quota counters; see §11.2). Upload pre-checks and post-upload increments use the Postgres-derived value during the outage window. If Postgres is also unavailable (dual-store outage), storage uploads are rejected with `503` (fail closed) — no bytes are written to MinIO, ensuring the storage quota can never be bypassed due to infrastructure degradation. On Redis recovery, the rehydrated counter is written back to Redis and normal fast-path enforcement resumes. |
| Circuit breakers (§11.6)   | **Fail closed (last-known state persists)** — each gateway replica retains the last-known circuit breaker state from its 5-second in-process cache. Open breakers remain enforced; no breaker can transition to closed without a confirmed Redis read. When Redis recovers, replicas re-read all circuit states from Redis and replace the cached snapshot. This ensures operator-initiated safety blocks are never silently lifted during infrastructure degradation. |
| Experiment sticky assignments | **Re-derive from deterministic hash (no data loss)** — the sticky cache is a performance optimization, not a correctness requirement (see §10.7). The bucketing algorithm (`HMAC-SHA256(experiment_id, assignment_key)`) is fully deterministic: given the same `(user_id, experiment_id)` pair, `assignVariant` always returns the same variant. On Redis unavailability, the gateway re-computes the assignment from the hash on each session creation instead of reading the cache. On Redis recovery, newly computed assignments are written back to the cache. No experiment statistical validity is affected because assignments are hash-derived, not randomly sampled and stored. |
| Delegation budget counters (`maxTreeSize`, `maxParallelChildren`, `maxTreeMemoryBytes`) | **Fail closed for new delegations** — `delegate_task` calls are rejected with a retryable `DELEGATION_BUDGET_UNAVAILABLE` error during the Redis outage window. Delegation trees are structurally complex to enforce in-memory across replicas, so fail-open is not safe for these counters. `maxDelegationDepth` enforcement is unaffected because it uses the Postgres-backed session lineage (not Redis counters) and remains enforced during Redis outages. In-flight child sessions already delegated before the outage continue running normally; only new `delegate_task` calls are blocked. On Redis recovery, delegation budget counters are reconstructed per the reconciliation procedure below before new delegations are accepted. |
| **Both stores down**       | **Dual-store degraded mode** — new sessions rejected (503), in-flight sessions continue on cached coordination state, `PLATFORM_DEGRADED` event emitted to clients, `DualStoreUnavailable` alert fires. See Section 10.1 ("Dual-store unavailability") for the full protocol. |

**Quota counter reconciliation after fail-open:** When Redis recovers, the gateway reconciles quota counters using a two-source MAX rule: for each active session and tenant counter, the gateway reads (1) the last Postgres checkpoint value and (2) the in-memory counter accumulated during the fail-open window on the recovering replica, then writes `MAX(postgres_checkpoint, in_memory_counter)` as the authoritative Redis value. Using the Postgres value alone would reset counters to a value up to `quotaSyncIntervalSeconds` (default 30s) stale — potentially un-enforcing a budget violation that occurred in the fail-open window. The MAX rule ensures that no usage accumulated during the fail-open window is lost. On session completion (terminal state), the gateway writes the final cumulative usage to Postgres as an authoritative reconciliation point, providing a durable baseline for future recoveries. Maximum drift during fail-open is bounded by the window duration (default 60s) multiplied by request rate — at 100 req/s per tenant, worst-case drift is ~6,000 requests. Worst-case drift scales with tier — see Section 17.8 for per-tier estimates. Reconciliation runs automatically when Redis becomes available and completes within seconds. During reconciliation, the gateway falls back to Postgres-backed counters (slower but accurate).

**Delegation budget counter reconciliation on Redis recovery:** On Redis recovery (restart or reconnection), the gateway additionally re-validates all active delegation tree budget counters before resuming delegation operations. The reconciliation procedure follows the two-source reconstruction described in Section 11.2 (Crash Recovery for Delegation Budget Counters): Postgres checkpoint + live pod enumeration, with irrecoverable trees moved to `awaiting_client_action`. This includes restoring the `maxTreeMemoryBytes` counter via `max(postgres_checkpoint_memory, liveMemoryBytes)` — without this step, a post-recovery counter of zero would allow trees already near their memory cap to accept new delegations they should have been blocked on. New `delegate_task` requests for any tree are rejected with a retryable error during the reconciliation window (typically < 5 seconds). The `lenny_delegation_budget_reconstruction_total` metric (labeled by `outcome`) is emitted for each tree processed, enabling operators to monitor reconstruction volume and detect irrecoverable trees. Operators should add this to the Redis recovery runbook (Section 17.7).

**Bounded fail-open for rate limiting:** When Redis becomes unavailable, the gateway starts a fail-open timer per replica. During the fail-open window, requests are allowed, a `rate_limit_degraded` metric is incremented, and an alert fires. After the window expires, if Redis is still unavailable, rate limiting fails **closed** — new requests are rejected with 429 until Redis recovers. **Emergency hard limit:** Each gateway replica maintains an in-memory per-user request counter as a coarse emergency backstop. This counter is not shared across replicas (so the effective limit is `N * per_replica_limit`) but prevents a single user from sending unlimited requests through one replica during the fail-open window. The `rateLimitFailOpenMaxSeconds` is configurable per deployment (default 60s). Higher tiers should use shorter fail-open windows to limit burst damage (Section 17.8).

**Per-user fail-open ceiling:** The per-tenant fail-open budget (see below) bounds aggregate tenant exposure but does not prevent a single user from monopolizing the entire tenant allocation during a Redis outage. Each gateway replica therefore maintains an additional **in-memory per-user token and request counter** active during the fail-open window. The per-user ceiling is `per_user_failopen_ceiling = min(tenant_limit * userFailOpenFraction, per_replica_hard_cap)`, where `userFailOpenFraction` is configurable per tenant (default: 0.25, i.e., a single user cannot consume more than 25% of the tenant's fail-open allocation on one replica). When a user's in-memory counter reaches `per_user_failopen_ceiling`, that user's requests are rejected with 429 for the remainder of the fail-open window, even if the per-tenant counter still has headroom. This prevents monopolization without affecting other users in the same tenant. The `userFailOpenFraction` is configurable via `quotaUserFailOpenFraction` in the gateway Helm values. Per-user fail-open counters are reset when Redis recovers and counters are reconciled using the standard MAX rule (see "Quota counter reconciliation after fail-open" below).

**Per-tenant fail-open budget enforcement:** In addition to per-user rate limiting, each gateway replica maintains in-memory per-tenant token and request counters that are active during Redis unavailability. Each replica also maintains a `cached_replica_count` variable (in-memory, not in Redis) that is updated every time the Kubernetes Endpoints object is successfully polled; this variable persists across individual poll failures so it always reflects the last-known good replica count. The per-replica ceiling for each tenant is `per_replica_limit = tenant_limit / max(cached_replica_count, 1)`, where `cached_replica_count` is the last successfully observed replica count sourced from the Kubernetes Endpoints object for the gateway Service (polled via the API server, not from Redis), with a maximum staleness of 30 seconds. If the Endpoints object has never been successfully polled (cold start), `cached_replica_count` defaults to `1`. A dual-outage (Redis + Endpoints simultaneously unavailable) will use the most-recently cached count rather than defaulting to `1`, preventing the `N × tenant_limit` overshoot that would otherwise occur if all replicas independently fell back to a count of 1. To further bound worst-case exposure, a hard per-replica cap applies regardless of replica count: `effective_ceiling = min(tenant_limit / max(cached_replica_count, 1), per_replica_hard_cap)`, where `per_replica_hard_cap` defaults to `tenant_limit / 2` and is configurable via `quotaPerReplicaHardCap`. Once a tenant's local counter reaches `effective_ceiling`, the replica rejects further requests for that tenant with 429 until Redis recovers. This ensures that even in the worst dual-outage case, total cluster-wide usage cannot exceed `min(N × (tenant_limit / max(cached_replica_count, 1)), N × per_replica_hard_cap)` — bounded well below `N × tenant_limit`. **Cumulative fail-open timer (security control):** Each replica tracks total cumulative time spent in fail-open mode via a true sliding window counter stored in-memory (not in Redis). The counter accumulates time across all Redis outages within the window — five separate 59-second outages each add 59 seconds to the running total, not just the most recent. If cumulative fail-open time exceeds `quotaFailOpenCumulativeMaxSeconds` (default: 300s) within any rolling 1-hour window (not calendar-aligned), the replica transitions to **fail-closed for quota enforcement** — all new sessions and token-consuming requests are blocked until Redis recovers and counters are reconciled. `quotaFailOpenCumulativeMaxSeconds` is a **financial security control**: its default (300s) should be reviewed per deployment's financial risk exposure and set to the maximum acceptable quota overshoot window. On replica restart, the cumulative timer is initialized from a local file (`/run/lenny/failopen-cumulative.json`) that each replica persists on every fail-open state transition. If the file exists and its timestamp is within the rolling 1-hour window, the cumulative counter resumes from the persisted value rather than resetting to zero. If the file is missing or corrupted (e.g., fresh pod on a new node), the timer resets to zero — this is the cold-start case and is acceptable because the replica has no prior fail-open history on this pod. This prevents the bypass scenario where a replica in CrashLoopBackOff during a sustained Redis outage would reset the cumulative timer on every restart and never reach the `quotaFailOpenCumulativeMaxSeconds` threshold. When a replica enters fail-open mode, it emits a `quota_failopen_started` audit event containing `tenant_id`, `replica_id`, and `timestamp`, enabling billing consumers to detect and attribute overshoot windows. The gateway also emits `quota_failopen_cumulative_seconds` (gauge) and fires alert `QuotaFailOpenCumulativeThreshold` when the cumulative timer exceeds 80% of the configured maximum.

**Scalability ceiling of single-instance Redis:** At Tiers 1 and 2, a single Redis Sentinel topology handles all Redis-backed concerns: session leases, quota counters, routing cache, token cache, and event pub/sub. This is simple to operate but creates a shared scalability ceiling — all concerns compete for the same CPU, memory, and network bandwidth on one primary node. Sentinel provides HA (automatic failover) but does not provide horizontal throughput scaling.

**When Sentinel becomes insufficient:** Monitor the following signals to determine when the single Sentinel topology is approaching its ceiling:

- **CPU saturation:** Redis primary sustained above 70% CPU (single-threaded; 70% of one core leaves limited headroom for latency-sensitive operations like lease renewals).
- **Memory pressure:** Used memory exceeds 75% of `maxmemory`, causing eviction risk for cached tokens and routing entries.
- **Operation latency:** P99 latency for `LeaseStore` or `QuotaStore` operations exceeds 5ms (baseline should be sub-1ms). Lease renewals are latency-critical — elevated latency risks false lease expirations.
- **Pub/sub fan-out cost:** At high session counts, pub/sub message delivery competes with key-value operations. If `redis_pubsub_channels` exceeds 5,000 and `redis_connected_subscribers` exceeds 10,000, pub/sub overhead becomes measurable.
- **Operations rate:** Approaching 80% of the budget operations estimate for the current tier (Section 17.8).
- **Delegation Lua aggregate blocking:** `lenny_redis_lua_script_duration_seconds{script="budget_reserve"}` P99 exceeds 2 ms sustained, or the cluster-wide instantaneous `delegate_task` rate exceeds 50/s — either condition indicates that cross-tenant `budget_reserve.lua` serialization is approaching the 5 ms `LeaseStore` SLO ceiling. See Section 8.3 ("Cross-tenant aggregate Lua contention") for the aggregate blocking formula and dedicated instance remediation steps.

**Logical separation of Redis concerns:** When the signals above indicate the single topology is insufficient (typically at upper Tier 2 or Tier 3 scale), separate Redis into independent instances by concern:

| Redis Instance            | Backed Concerns                                     | Scaling Rationale                                                                                      |
| ------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Coordination**          | `LeaseStore` (session leases), routing cache        | Latency-critical, low throughput, small key space. Sentinel topology is sufficient even at Tier 3.     |
| **Quota / Rate Limiting** | `QuotaStore` (rate limit counters, budget tracking) | High write throughput (per-request increments). Benefits most from Redis Cluster sharding at scale.    |
| **Cache / Pub-Sub**       | Token cache, event pub/sub                          | Tolerates higher latency. Pub/sub fan-out is CPU-intensive and should not compete with lease renewals. |

At Tiers 1 and 2, all concerns run on a single Sentinel instance. The separation is a deployment-time configuration change (separate connection strings per store role) — no code changes are required because each store role already has its own interface (Section 12.6). See Section 17.8 for per-tier Redis topology recommendations including when to split.

**Tier 3 Redis write throughput quantification:** At Tier 3 scale (~10,000 concurrent sessions, 2,000 new sessions/s), the estimated write load on the Quota/Rate Limiting Redis instance is the dominant concern. The principal write sources and their rates are:

| Write Source                            | Estimated ops/s (Tier 3) | Notes                                                                         |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Quota counter increments (`INCR`)       | ~2,000/s per active tenant | Per-request token or session counter increment; one `INCR` per gateway→pod RPC |
| Rate limit counter increments           | ~2,000/s                 | One `INCR` per inbound request per tenant                                     |
| Delegation budget decrements (Lua)      | ~500–1,000/s             | Multi-tenant aggregate Redis ops/s (not per-script invocation rate — each Lua invocation executes ~10–15 internal Redis commands; see §16.5 reconciliation). At burst rates ≥ 50 concurrent scripts, aggregate serialization blocking exceeds 5 ms — see cross-tenant contention analysis in Section 8.3. |
| Lease renewals (`SET` with TTL)         | ~1,000/s                 | Per-session lease heartbeat; one per coordination TTL / 2                     |
| Token cache writes (`SET NX`)           | ~500/s                   | Cold-miss re-fetches from TokenStore                                           |
| **Total estimated write ops/s**         | **~6,000–6,500/s sustained** | Burst at session storm may reach ~20,000/s                                |

A single-threaded Redis primary can sustain ~100,000–200,000 simple ops/s on modern hardware. The ~6,500/s sustained Tier 3 write rate is within a Redis Cluster topology's capacity but **is NOT trivially safe on a single Sentinel primary** at Tier 3 scale — CPU contention from concurrent read operations, pub/sub fan-out, and Lua script serialization (Section 8.3) combine with write throughput to approach single-primary limits. The Tier 3 Redis Cluster topology recommendation in Section 17.8 is required for sustained Tier 3 write rates; Sentinel is appropriate only through Tier 2. The `~100,000 ops/s` budget estimate in Section 17.8 accounts for total operation mix (reads + writes + pub/sub); the ceiling signals in the **"When Sentinel becomes insufficient"** block above are the operational decision criteria.

**Redis Cluster migration pre-plan (Tier 2→3 transition):** For deployments targeting Tier 3 scale, Redis Cluster migration for the Quota/Rate Limiting instance must be planned **before** the Tier 2→3 promotion decision — not reactively once ceiling signals have already fired in production. The migration procedure is:

1. **Trigger:** Initiate planning when any two ceiling signals (CPU > 70%, P99 latency > 5ms, ops rate > 80% of budget) are simultaneously active for more than 30 minutes at Tier 2 peak load.
2. **Topology target:** Redis Cluster with minimum 6 nodes (3 primary + 3 replica shards), using hash-slot-based sharding. The `t:{tenant_id}:` key prefix convention (Section 12.4) ensures all keys for a given tenant hash to the same slot, preserving multi-key operation correctness for per-tenant Lua scripts.
3. **Interface compatibility:** No application code changes are required. Each store role interface (Section 12.6) already abstracts the Redis client. The `QuotaStore` and rate limiter implementations must use `CLUSTER KEYSLOT`-aware client libraries (e.g., `go-redis/v9` with cluster mode) — verify this before migration.
4. **Migration steps:** (a) Deploy Cluster in parallel with the existing Sentinel instance; (b) migrate tenants in groups using a feature flag (`redis.quotaStoreBackend: sentinel | cluster`) settable per-tenant; (c) validate quota and rate limit correctness for migrated tenants for 24h before proceeding; (d) migrate remaining tenants; (e) decommission Sentinel instance for Quota/Rate Limiting. The Coordination instance remains on Sentinel throughout — Cluster is not required for low-throughput coordination workloads.
5. **Rollback:** The per-tenant feature flag allows individual rollback to Sentinel if correctness issues are observed. Any quota counters that diverged between the two stores during the migration window must be reconciled by re-reading from Postgres (same procedure as fail-open reconciliation above).

**In-memory quota budgets with Postgres reconciliation:** For high-value limits (e.g., per-tenant token budgets where overshoot has direct cost impact), each gateway replica can maintain an in-memory budget allocation drawn from Postgres rather than relying solely on Redis counters. The replica requests a budget slice from Postgres on startup (e.g., 1/N of the tenant's remaining budget, where N is the replica count), decrements locally per request, and reconciles with Postgres periodically (default: every 30s) or when the local slice is 80% consumed. This approach tolerates full Redis unavailability for quota enforcement with bounded overshoot (at most one slice per replica). It is **not the default** — deployers enable it via `quotaEnforcementMode: in_memory_reconciled` when Redis-based quota drift during outages is unacceptable.

### 12.5 Artifact Store

**Backend:** Any S3-compatible object store. Cloud-managed deployments should use the provider's native object storage (AWS S3, GCP Cloud Storage, Azure Blob Storage) — see Section 17.9 for deployment profile guidance. Self-managed deployments use MinIO. For local development, use local disk with the same interface.

**HA topology requirements:**

- **Production topology:** MinIO with erasure coding (minimum 4 nodes, recommended 4+ nodes across availability zones) for data durability. See Section 17.8 for per-tier MinIO sizing.
- **Versioning:** Enable bucket versioning for checkpoint objects to prevent accidental overwrites
- **Replication:** For multi-zone deployments, configure MinIO site-to-site replication for near-zero RPO on artifact data
- **TLS:** MinIO connections MUST use TLS (`https://` endpoint). The Helm chart sets `minio.endpoint` to `https://minio.lenny-system:9000` by default. TLS may be disabled in local development mode via `minio.tls.enabled: false` in Helm values; this value defaults to `false` only when `global.deploymentProfile: dev`. All other deployment profiles enforce TLS.
- **Delete-marker lifecycle:** Bucket versioning is bucket-wide, and the GC job (below) deletes artifacts across all object prefixes — not just checkpoints. Each GC deletion on a versioned bucket creates a delete marker. Therefore, the delete-marker expiration and noncurrent-version expiration lifecycle rules MUST apply bucket-wide (not scoped to a single prefix). The rules are: expire delete markers after 24 hours, and expire noncurrent versions after 1 day (`NoncurrentVersionExpiration: Days: 1`). This prevents delete-marker accumulation from degrading `ListObjects` performance at scale across all prefixes (checkpoints, uploads, transcripts, eviction context, workspace snapshots). The Helm chart post-install Job configures this rule via `mc ilm add` with no prefix filter (bucket-wide scope).

**MinIO unavailability during checkpoint:** If MinIO is unreachable during a checkpoint upload, the adapter retries with exponential backoff as described in Section 4.4. Deployers should monitor the `CheckpointStorageUnavailable` critical alert and the `lenny_checkpoint_storage_failure_total` metric. Prolonged MinIO unavailability degrades session durability — any pod eviction during an outage that exhausts all MinIO retries falls back to the Postgres minimal state record (Section 4.4); sessions resume with conversation context but workspace state is lost.

**Risk: simultaneous MinIO outage and node eviction.** A MinIO rolling upgrade or network partition coinciding with a node drain causes simultaneous preStop hooks across all pods on the drained node, all competing for an unavailable MinIO, all falling back to minimal state. This is a known cascading failure scenario. Mitigations: (1) the `CheckpointStale` alert (Section 16.5) fires before eviction if the freshness SLO is already missed, giving operators an early signal; (2) deployers should stagger node drains — drain one node at a time (use `kubectl drain --max-unavailable 1` or cluster-autoscaler drain rate limits) to limit the number of simultaneous eviction checkpoints; (3) schedule MinIO maintenance windows outside of cluster upgrade windows to avoid the combination. The Postgres minimal state fallback is the last-resort safety net, not a substitute for operational discipline around MinIO availability.

**Pre-drain MinIO health check (planned drains only).** This webhook protects against planned drains (operator-initiated `kubectl drain`, cluster-autoscaler scale-downs via the eviction API) only. It cannot protect against spontaneous node failures, OOM kills, or preemptions, which bypass the eviction API entirely. For spontaneous failures, the `CheckpointStale` alert and the periodic checkpoint freshness SLO (Section 4.4) are the primary defenses against data loss. To prevent draining a node into a known-degraded MinIO state, the gateway exposes a **drain readiness endpoint**: `GET /internal/drain-readiness`. Before any operator or automation initiates a `kubectl drain` on a node, a pre-drain hook MUST query this endpoint. The endpoint performs a MinIO liveness probe (S3 `HeadBucket` on the checkpoints bucket with a 2-second timeout) and returns:

- `HTTP 200 {"status": "ready", "minio": "healthy"}` — drain may proceed.
- `HTTP 503 {"status": "not_ready", "minio": "unhealthy", "reason": "<error>"}` — drain MUST be deferred until MinIO recovers.

The pre-drain hook is implemented as a Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionWebhook` named `lenny-drain-readiness` that intercepts `CREATE` operations on pod eviction objects (resource: `pods/eviction`) in the agent namespaces. When the webhook fires, it calls the gateway's `/internal/drain-readiness` endpoint. If the endpoint returns `503` or is unreachable, the webhook rejects the eviction with: `"Node drain blocked: MinIO health check failed — defer drain until MinIO is healthy (STR-006)"`. The webhook is deployed with `failurePolicy: Fail` so that webhook unavailability also blocks drain, preventing silent data loss paths. For cluster-autoscaler-triggered drains, the autoscaler's eviction flow passes through the same Kubernetes eviction API and is therefore subject to the same webhook. Operators can bypass the check for emergency drains by annotating the node with `lenny.dev/drain-force: "true"` and providing justification — the webhook permits the drain but emits a `node.drain.forced` critical audit event. Metric: `lenny_drain_readiness_checks_total` (counter, labeled by `outcome`: `allowed`, `blocked`, `forced`) tracks drain check outcomes.

**Do not use Postgres for blob storage.** Workspace checkpoints (up to 500MB) cause TOAST overhead, vacuum pressure, and degrade transactional workload performance.

**Tenant isolation:** All object keys use the path format `/{tenant_id}/{object_type}/{session_id}/{filename}`. The `ArtifactStore` implementation validates the `tenant_id` prefix on every operation — no S3 call is issued unless the caller's authenticated `tenant_id` matches the path prefix. This validation lives at the interface boundary, not in individual callers. `DeleteByTenant(tenant_id)` performs a prefix-scoped bulk delete on `/{tenant_id}/*`. The GC job (below) inherits the same prefix scoping: it only deletes artifacts under the tenant prefix associated with each expired record.

**Encryption at rest:** All stored objects (checkpoints, workspace snapshots, session transcripts) contain sensitive data including conversation history and workspace file contents. MinIO server-side encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS) must be enabled for production deployments. For cloud deployments using managed object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob), encryption at rest is typically enabled by default. For self-hosted MinIO, enable SSE with a KMS backend (HashiCorp Vault, AWS KMS) or at minimum SSE-S3 with MinIO's internal key management. **T4 tenant encryption:** For tenants with `workspaceTier: T4` (Section 12.9), workspace snapshots and session transcripts in object storage **must** use SSE-KMS with a tenant-specific KMS key — not the default shared encryption key. This enables per-tenant key rotation and cryptographic erasure (revoking the tenant's KMS key renders all their stored artifacts unreadable). The `ArtifactStore` implementation selects the encryption key based on the tenant's `workspaceTier`: T3 uses the deployment-wide SSE key; T4 uses a KMS key scoped to `tenant:{tenant_id}`. Key provisioning is the deployer's responsibility (e.g., AWS KMS key per tenant, HashiCorp Vault transit key per tenant). The preflight check (Section 17.6) validates that MinIO SSE is enabled but does not validate per-tenant KMS key existence — this is validated at runtime when the first T4 artifact is written, returning `CLASSIFICATION_CONTROL_VIOLATION` if the tenant-scoped KMS key is unavailable.

**Storage quota enforcement.** Per-tenant storage quotas are enforced via a pre-upload size check, post-upload Redis counter increment, and GC-triggered decrement, as defined in Section 11.2 (Storage quota enforcement mechanism). The `artifact_store` Postgres table stores `artifact_size_bytes` for every artifact; the GC job uses this to decrement the Redis counter on deletion and to rehydrate counters on Redis restart. This table covers **all** MinIO-backed artifacts, including workspace snapshots, checkpoints, uploaded files, session transcripts, and eviction context objects (see Section 4.4 for the `artifact_store` insert requirement for the context > 2KB path). The `lenny_storage_quota_bytes_used` gauge tracks per-tenant consumption. Alert: `StorageQuotaHigh` fires at 80% utilization.

**Checkpoint retention policy:**

- Keep only the latest 2 checkpoints per active session. **Exception: sessions with `legal_hold = true` are exempt from this rotation — all checkpoints are retained until the hold is lifted (see Section 12.8 Legal hold and checkpoint rotation).** In concurrent-workspace mode (Section 5.2), checkpoints are per-slot — the "latest 2" limit applies independently to each slot, meaning a pod with `maxConcurrent: 8` may retain up to 16 checkpoint objects (8 slots × 2). The GC job and retention policy operate on `(session_id, slot_id)` pairs, not on sessions alone.
- Delete all checkpoints when session terminates and resume window expires
- **Eviction context objects:** When a session reaches a terminal state, the gateway deletes the MinIO eviction context object (`/{tenant_id}/eviction/{session_id}/context`) as part of terminal-state cleanup (Section 4.4). Any objects that escape this primary deletion path (e.g., gateway crash before cleanup) are picked up by the GC sweep: the GC job queries `session_eviction_state` rows for sessions that have reached a terminal state and whose `last_message_context` column stores a MinIO object key (identified by the `/{tenant_id}/eviction/` key prefix), deletes the corresponding MinIO objects, and removes the Postgres rows. This GC path runs within the same cycle as other artifact cleanup.
- **Partial checkpoint manifests:** Partial checkpoint manifest records (`partial: true` in the checkpoint metadata table) are normally deleted immediately after resume completes (Section 4.4). The GC job provides a backstop sweep for manifests that escape the primary cleanup path: on each cycle the GC job queries all rows where `partial = true` and either (a) the session has reached a terminal state, or (b) the row's `created_at` timestamp is older than `maxResumeWindowSeconds` (i.e., the resume window has expired and no resume can consume this manifest). For each such row, the GC job deletes all referenced MinIO parts (using `AbortMultipartUpload` for open multipart upload IDs and `DeleteObject` for individually committed parts), then removes the Postgres row. This sweep prevents partial MinIO parts from becoming permanently orphaned when the gateway crashes before performing cleanup or when a session is never resumed within its resume window.
- Background GC job runs every 15 minutes to clean expired artifacts. The interval is configurable via the `gc.cycleIntervalSeconds` Helm value (default: 900, minimum: 60). The job is owned by the gateway — it runs as a leader-elected goroutine inside the gateway process (not a separate CronJob). Only one gateway instance runs GC at a time via the existing leader-election lease. Higher tiers may require more frequent cycles (Section 17.8). Tenants with `gcPriority: high` (configurable per tenant via the admin API, intended for T4 erasure SLA compliance) trigger an immediate incremental GC sweep scoped to that tenant whenever an erasure job completes, independent of the global cycle interval.
- The GC job is idempotent: it queries Postgres for artifacts past their TTL, deletes them from MinIO, marks the rows as `deleted` in Postgres (commit), and only then decrements the Redis storage quota counter. This ordering is mandatory — the Redis decrement must follow the Postgres commit (see Section 11.2 step 3 for rationale: decrementing before commit creates a double-decrement on crash recovery because rehydration re-adds bytes for rows that are still `active` in Postgres). Re-running on the same set is safe: MinIO delete-on-absent is a no-op, the Postgres update is conditional on current state (`WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`), and a Redis decrement on an already-deleted artifact is prevented because the Postgres query only returns rows where `deleted_at IS NULL`.
- **No reference counting required in v1:** Each MinIO artifact object is owned by exactly one session. Session derivation (Section 7.1) always copies the parent's workspace snapshot into a new session-scoped MinIO object — no object is shared across sessions. TTL-based deletion is therefore safe: deleting a parent's expired artifact cannot affect any derived session's workspace. Reference counting will be required only if a future content-addressed deduplication optimization is introduced (see Section 12.8).
- Individual artifact deletion failures are retried independently — one stuck artifact does not block cleanup of others. On cycle failure the job logs the error, increments the error counter, and retries on the next 15-minute cycle.
- **Monitoring:** `lenny_gc_runs_total` (counter), `lenny_gc_artifacts_deleted` (counter), `lenny_gc_errors_total` (counter), `lenny_gc_duration_seconds` (histogram). Alert rule `ArtifactGCBacklog` (Section 16.5) fires when expired artifacts pending cleanup exceeds 1 000.
- Session artifact retention: configurable TTL (default 7 days), extendable per session

### 12.6 Interface Design

Good:

```
SessionStore.claim_session(session_id, gateway_id)
SessionStore.mark_session_attached(session_id, pod_id)
TokenStore.save_refresh_token(user_id, connector_id, encrypted_token)
QuotaStore.increment_token_usage(user_id, window, tokens_used)
```

Bad:

```
Database.query("UPDATE sessions SET ...")
GenericStore.put(key, value)
```

**Scaling extension interfaces.** The following five interfaces are defined in v1 with trivial single-node implementations. They exist to ensure that the components they abstract can be swapped at Tier 4 scale without restructuring the platform core. Each interface ships with exactly one v1 implementation; adding a Tier 4 implementation requires no application code restructuring — the swap is a configuration-only change at the store-router level. Note: some Tier 4 implementations require accompanying infrastructure changes beyond configuration (e.g., `PostgresPodRegistry` requires the `agent_pod_state` schema migration; `NATSEventBus`/`KafkaEventBus` requires deploying and operating a new message broker). All five interfaces are in the `platform/store` package.

**Shared type definitions.** The five interfaces below reference common ID types, enums, and struct types. The following code block defines the shared types; struct types referenced in method signatures but not defined here (e.g., `PodRecord`, `PodSpec`) are summarized in the cross-reference table that follows.

```go
// --- Shared types used by the five scaling extension interfaces ---

// ID types — typed strings for compile-time safety.
type PodID     string
type PoolID    string
type TenantID  string
type SessionID string
type ClusterID string

// RedisConcern selects which Redis instance StoreRouter returns.
// In v1, all concerns map to a single Redis instance.
// At Tier 3+, operators may split concerns to separate instances
// for isolation and independent scaling.
type RedisConcern string
const (
    RedisConcernCoordination RedisConcern = "coordination"   // session leases, generation counters
    RedisConcernQuota        RedisConcern = "quota"           // token/rate counters, sliding windows, billing stream
    RedisConcernCachePubSub  RedisConcern = "cache_pubsub"    // routing cache, EventBus channels, semantic cache, experiment sticky assignments
    RedisConcernDelegation   RedisConcern = "delegation"      // budget keys ({root_session_id}:dlg:*)
    RedisConcernSessionData  RedisConcern = "session_data"    // DLQ, durable inbox
    // Note: delegation is co-located with quota until Tier 3 contention
    // justifies separation (see §17.8 ops/s estimates).
    // session_data is co-located with coordination in v1.
)

// StoreType identifies a Postgres store category for ShardCount.
type StoreType string
const (
    StoreTypeSession StoreType = "session"  // sessions, session_messages, session_tree_archive
    StoreTypeTenant  StoreType = "tenant"   // tenants, environments, runtime_definitions, credential_pools, delegation_policies
    StoreTypeBilling StoreType = "billing"  // billing_events (R-03)
    StoreTypeAudit   StoreType = "audit"    // audit_log (R-03)
    // Note: billing and audit share the same DSN in v1 but have
    // separate routing methods to allow independent sharding later.
)

// ReleaseReason indicates why a pod is being released.
type ReleaseReason string
const (
    ReleaseReasonSessionComplete ReleaseReason = "session_complete"
    ReleaseReasonSessionFailed   ReleaseReason = "session_failed"
    ReleaseReasonEviction        ReleaseReason = "eviction"
    ReleaseReasonDrain           ReleaseReason = "drain"
)

// Subscription is returned by EventBus.Subscribe.
type Subscription interface {
    Unsubscribe() error
}
```

**Struct cross-reference table.** The following struct types appear in interface method signatures. Each is defined by the section listed; the fields column summarizes the key fields for implementor orientation.

| Struct | Defined in | Key fields |
|---|---|---|
| `PodRecord` | §4.6.1 (pod state machine) | `PodID`, `PoolID`, `TenantID`, `State`, `SessionID` (nullable), `IsolationProfile`, `ExecutionMode`, `ResourceVersion` (CRD optimistic-locking generation) |
| `PodSpec` | §4.6.1 (pod creation) | `RuntimeDefinitionRef`, `WorkspacePlan`, `IsolationProfile`, `ExecutionMode`, resource limits |
| `StateTransition` | §4.6.1 (pod state machine) | source state, target state, reason; validated against the pod state machine |
| `ClaimOpts` | §4.6.1, §12.6 | `PoolID`, `TenantID`, `SessionID`, `RequiresDemotion` (bool), `Priority` (nullable `*int32`, nil when unset), `ClusterID` (nullable `*ClusterID`, always nil in v1) |
| `PodFilter` | §12.6 (PodRegistry) | state filter, label selectors; implementation-defined |
| `StateCounts` | §12.6 (PodRegistry) | map of pod state → count; used by `PoolScalingController` for scaling decisions |
| `PodEvent` | §12.6 (PodRegistry) | `PodID`, `EventType` (created/updated/deleted), `PodRecord` snapshot |
| `ShardHandle` | §12.6 (StoreRouter) | shard identifier + `*pgxpool.Pool`; returned by `AllSessionShards` for scatter-gather |
| `ClusterInfo` | §12.6 (ClusterRegistry) | `ClusterID`, endpoint, `CACertBundle` (remote CA chain for mTLS verification), capacity metadata, health status |
| `ClusterSelectionRequest` | §12.6 (ClusterRegistry) | affinity hints, resource requirements; v1 implementation ignores all fields and returns `LocalClusterID()` |
| `CredentialRequest` | §12.6 (CredentialGenerator) | `TenantID`, `Provider`, `PoolID`, required scopes |
| `CredentialLease` | §4.9 (credential leasing) | `LeaseID`, `SessionID`, `Provider`, `PoolID`, `CredentialID`, `MaterializedConfig` (provider-specific), `ExpiresAt`, `RenewBefore`, `FallbackAllowed`, `Source` (pool/user) — full JSON schema in §4.9 |

#### PodRegistry

Abstracts pod lifecycle state as a data-access layer. The §4.6.1 business-logic interfaces (`PodLifecycleManager`, `PoolManager`) delegate their state reads and writes to `PodRegistry` — for example, `PodLifecycleManager.ClaimPod` runs the optimistic-locking CAS loop and `SandboxClaim` creation, calling `PodRegistry.ClaimPod` for the atomic pod-record state transition. Method names overlap intentionally: PodRegistry provides the storage primitive; the §4.6.1 interfaces add domain logic (session binding, checkpoint coordination, pool reconciliation) on top. The v1 implementation (`CRDPodRegistry`) reads and writes `Sandbox` CRD status via the Kubernetes API. At Tier 4, `PostgresPodRegistry` replaces CRD status writes with the `agent_pod_state` Postgres table, eliminating ~95% of etcd write pressure (see §4.6.1 etcd write pressure table).

```go
type PodRegistry interface {
    GetPod(ctx context.Context, podID PodID) (*PodRecord, error)
    UpdatePodState(ctx context.Context, podID PodID, transition StateTransition) error
    ClaimPod(ctx context.Context, opts ClaimOpts) (*PodRecord, error)
    ReleasePod(ctx context.Context, podID PodID, reason ReleaseReason) error
    ListPodsByPool(ctx context.Context, poolID PoolID, filter PodFilter) ([]PodRecord, error)
    CountByState(ctx context.Context, poolID PoolID) (StateCounts, error)
    CreatePod(ctx context.Context, poolID PoolID, spec PodSpec) (*PodRecord, error)
    DeletePod(ctx context.Context, podID PodID) error
    WatchPods(ctx context.Context, poolID PoolID) (<-chan PodEvent, error)
}
// v1: CRDPodRegistry (Kubernetes API)
```

`ClaimOpts.ClusterID` is a nullable field (type `*ClusterID`). In v1, `ClaimOpts.ClusterID` is always `nil` (single-cluster). The `LocalClusterRegistry` (see below) propagates a non-nil `ClusterID` when routing claims to a remote cluster in a multi-cluster topology.

**`agent_pod_state` table schema.** The `agent_pod_state` Postgres table mirrors `Sandbox` CRD status fields. In v1 it is a read-optimized mirror maintained by the WarmPoolController on every pod state transition; at Tier 4 it becomes the primary store for `PostgresPodRegistry`. The table is platform-global (no RLS — `tenant_id` is a denormalized convenience column set on claim, not an isolation boundary; see §4.2 data model table).

```sql
CREATE TABLE agent_pod_state (
    pod_id          TEXT        PRIMARY KEY,
    pool_id         TEXT        NOT NULL,
    state           TEXT        NOT NULL,  -- pod state machine value (idle, claimed, running, draining, terminated, failed)
    tenant_id       TEXT,                  -- NULL when idle; set on claim alongside session_id (denormalized for query convenience)
    session_id      TEXT,                  -- NULL when idle; set on claim
    isolation_profile TEXT      NOT NULL,  -- standard, sandboxed, microvm
    execution_mode  TEXT        NOT NULL,  -- session, task, concurrent_workspace
    resource_version BIGINT    NOT NULL,   -- CRD generation for optimistic-locking; incremented on every state transition
    node_name       TEXT,                  -- Kubernetes node hosting the pod
    updated_at      TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_agent_pod_state_pool_state ON agent_pod_state (pool_id, state);
CREATE INDEX idx_agent_pod_state_session    ON agent_pod_state (session_id) WHERE session_id IS NOT NULL;
CREATE INDEX idx_agent_pod_state_tenant     ON agent_pod_state (tenant_id) WHERE tenant_id IS NOT NULL;
```

The `pool_id + state` index supports `ListPodsByPool` with state filter and `CountByState`. The partial `session_id` index supports the orphan session reconciler cross-reference (§10.1). `resource_version` is used by `UpdatePodState` for optimistic-locking CAS: `UPDATE ... SET state = $new, resource_version = resource_version + 1 WHERE pod_id = $id AND resource_version = $expected`. The `updated_at` column drives the `lenny_agent_pod_state_mirror_lag_seconds` staleness gauge (§10.1).

**Observability contract.** Every `PodRegistry` implementation MUST emit: `lenny_pod_registry_operation_duration_seconds{operation, pool}` (histogram) and `lenny_pod_registry_error_total{operation, pool}` (counter). The `operation` label is one of: `get`, `update_state`, `claim`, `release`, `list`, `count`, `create`, `delete`, `watch`. These metrics provide per-operation visibility for implementation diagnosis (e.g., detecting a slow `PostgresPodRegistry` at Tier 4). The existing pod claim metrics (`lenny_pod_claim_queue_*`, `lenny_pod_claim_conflict_total`, `lenny_pod_claim_timeout_total`) remain as higher-level business metrics capturing the end-to-end claim flow including CAS retries and queue wait time; the registry-level metrics capture the underlying storage operation only.

**`WatchPods` delivery contract.** `WatchPods` provides **eventually-consistent change notification**, not a reliable event stream. Implementations MUST deliver events within a bounded latency: 500 ms P99 for `CRDPodRegistry` (Kubernetes informer propagation), 2 s P99 for a future `PostgresPodRegistry` (poll or LISTEN/NOTIFY propagation). Implementations MAY coalesce or drop events under backpressure — the channel is a hint, not a ledger. Consumers MUST NOT rely on `WatchPods` as the sole source of truth; they MUST periodically reconcile via `CountByState` or `ListPodsByPool` on a configurable interval (default: 10 s for `PoolScalingController`). The reconciliation interval governs correctness; the watch channel governs responsiveness.

The channel MUST NOT emit an initial state snapshot on creation. Consumers call `ListPodsByPool` for initial state, then `WatchPods` for subsequent deltas. If the implementation detects that the channel has fallen behind (e.g., a Postgres poll missed intermediate states), it SHOULD send a synthetic `PodEvent` with `EventType = resync` carrying no `PodRecord` — the consumer treats this as a signal to re-read via `ListPodsByPool`.

**`PostgresPodRegistry` implementation guidance.** A future `PostgresPodRegistry` implementation SHOULD use Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` on a per-pool channel (`pod_state_change_{pool_id}`) triggered by an `AFTER UPDATE` trigger on `agent_pod_state`. The trigger sends the `pod_id` as the notification payload; the `WatchPods` goroutine reads the full `PodRecord` on receipt. If `LISTEN/NOTIFY` is unavailable (e.g., connection-pooler limitation with PgBouncer in transaction mode), the implementation should fall back to polling `agent_pod_state WHERE pool_id = $1 AND updated_at > $last_poll` at 1 s intervals. The `lenny_pod_registry_watch_lag_seconds{pool, implementation}` gauge tracks the delay between `updated_at` on the row and the time the event is delivered to the channel — this metric is required for all `PodRegistry` implementations that support `WatchPods`.

#### StoreRouter

Abstracts Postgres shard routing and per-concern Redis instance selection. The v1 implementation (`SingleShardRouter`) returns the sole configured connection pool for every call. All billing and audit writes MUST be routed through `StoreRouter.BillingShard` and `StoreRouter.AuditShard` even in v1 (see §12.3 R-03).

```go
type StoreRouter interface {
    // TenantShard returns the pool for tenant-scoped metadata tables
    // (tenants, environments, runtime_definitions, credential_pools,
    // delegation_policies). Always routes by tenant_id.
    TenantShard(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID) (*pgxpool.Pool, error)

    // SessionShard returns the pool for session-scoped tables
    // (sessions, session_messages, session_tree_archive).
    // Extracts the shard routing prefix from the session ID
    // (see session ID format below) and consistent-hashes it.
    // All sessions in a delegation tree share the same routing
    // prefix, so they co-locate on the same shard.
    // In v1, returns the same pool as TenantShard.
    SessionShard(ctx context.Context, sessionID SessionID) (*pgxpool.Pool, error)

    // BillingShard returns the pool for billing event writes.
    // Always routes by tenant_id (R-03).
    BillingShard(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID) (*pgxpool.Pool, error)

    // AuditShard returns the pool for audit log writes.
    // Always routes by tenant_id (R-03).
    AuditShard(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID) (*pgxpool.Pool, error)

    // RedisShard returns the Redis client for a given concern.
    RedisShard(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID, concern RedisConcern) (redis.UniversalClient, error)

    // PlatformRedis returns the Redis client for platform-scoped keys
    // (pod slot counters, circuit breakers). Not tenant-routed.
    // In v1, returns the same instance as all RedisConcern values.
    PlatformRedis(ctx context.Context) (redis.UniversalClient, error)

    // AllSessionShards returns all session-table shards for scatter-gather
    // queries (e.g., list sessions for a tenant, GDPR erasure).
    AllSessionShards(ctx context.Context) ([]ShardHandle, error)

    // ShardCount returns the number of shards for a given store type.
    ShardCount(storeType StoreType) int
}
// v1: SingleShardRouter (single Postgres pool, single Redis per concern)
```

**Sharding strategy.** The StoreRouter separates two routing dimensions: tenant-scoped metadata (`TenantShard`) and session-scoped data (`SessionShard`). In v1 (`SingleShardRouter`), both return the same pool. A future session-sharded topology distributes session-scoped tables by consistent hash of the **shard routing prefix** embedded in each session ID (see session ID format below). All sessions in a delegation tree share the same routing prefix, so they co-locate on the same shard — enabling single-shard tree traversal, cascade cancellation, and delegation lineage queries. Standalone sessions (no delegation) have random routing prefixes and distribute uniformly. Tree sizes are bounded by `maxChildrenTotal` and `maxDepth` delegation policy limits, so no single tree creates a hot shard. Tenant-scoped metadata tables remain tenant-routed — their volume is low and they don't create hot shards.

**Caller guide:** All single-session operations use `SessionShard(sessionID)` — the shard routing prefix is embedded in every session ID, so any session ID (root or child) routes correctly without external lookups. This covers session creation, single-session reads, coordinator handoff, and admin API lookups. Tenant-wide session queries (`GET /v1/sessions`, GDPR erasure, tenant deletion) use `AllSessionShards()` with a `tenant_id` filter on each shard. Tenant config reads (environments, policies, credential pools) use `TenantShard(tenantID)`. Billing and audit writes use `BillingShard` and `AuditShard` per R-03.

**Scatter-gather execution constraints.** All scatter-gather queries using `AllSessionShards()` MUST observe the following bounds. In v1, `SingleShardRouter.AllSessionShards()` returns a single `ShardHandle`, so these constraints are trivially satisfied; they become load-bearing only when a multi-shard `StoreRouter` implementation is deployed.

- **Concurrency limit.** At most `storeRouter.maxScatterGatherConcurrency` shards are queried in parallel (Helm value, default: 16). Remaining shards are queried in subsequent batches. This prevents a tenant-wide query from saturating all shard connection pools simultaneously.
- **Per-shard timeout.** Each shard query has a deadline of `storeRouter.scatterGatherPerShardTimeoutSeconds` (Helm value, default: 10 s). If a shard times out, the scatter-gather operation logs a `scatter_gather_shard_timeout` structured event (including shard ID and query type) and either (a) returns a partial result with a `partial: true` flag for read paths (list sessions), or (b) retries the timed-out shard up to 2 times for write paths (GDPR erasure, tenant deletion) before failing the operation.
- **Aggregate timeout.** The total scatter-gather operation has a deadline of `storeRouter.scatterGatherAggregateTimeoutSeconds` (Helm value, default: 120 s). This caps worst-case latency when many shards are slow.

Every scatter-gather invocation MUST emit `lenny_store_router_scatter_gather_duration_seconds{query_type}` (histogram) and `lenny_store_router_scatter_gather_shard_count{query_type}` (gauge). The `query_type` label is one of: `list_sessions`, `gdpr_erasure`, `tenant_deletion`, `delegation_budget_purge`. A `ScatterGatherSlowQuery` warning alert fires when P99 of `lenny_store_router_scatter_gather_duration_seconds` exceeds 30 s sustained for 5 minutes.

`SessionShard` implementations MUST resolve within 5 ms P99 — this call is on the coordinator handoff hot path.

**Session ID format.** Session IDs are UUIDv8 (RFC 9562) with an application-defined bit layout that embeds a **shard routing prefix** in the first 32 bits:

```
xxxxxxxx-xxxx-8xxx-yxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
^^^^^^^^      ^    ^
routing       ver  variant
prefix        (8)  (10xx)
(32 bits)
```

The routing prefix determines the Postgres shard for session-scoped tables. `SessionShard()` extracts the first 32 bits from any session ID and consistent-hashes them — no external state or lookup is needed. Generation rules:

- **Root session creation:** The gateway generates a UUIDv8 with a fully random routing prefix (32 random bits). The remaining ~90 bits (after the version nibble and variant bits) are also random.
- **Child session creation (delegation):** The gateway copies the first 32 bits from the root session's UUID into the child's UUID. The remaining bits are random. This guarantees every session in a delegation tree shares the same routing prefix and lands on the same shard. The `root_session_id` used for prefix copying is available from the delegation lease (§8.3 R-04).
- **Session derivation (`/v1/sessions/{id}/derive`):** The derived session is a new independent root — its UUID gets a fully random routing prefix. The derived session forms a new tree on a potentially different shard.

With 32 bits for routing and ~90 random bits for uniqueness, collision probability within a single delegation tree reaches 50% at ~2^45 children. Tree sizes are bounded by policy at hundreds — collision risk is effectively zero.

External consumers (clients, admin API callers, MCP tooling) treat session IDs as opaque strings. The routing prefix is an internal implementation concern and is not exposed in any API contract.

#### ClusterRegistry

Abstracts cluster topology for multi-cluster delegation routing. The v1 implementation (`LocalClusterRegistry`) exposes only the local cluster and always returns `LocalClusterID()` from `SelectCluster`.

```go
// RemotePodOperations is the subset of PodRegistry permitted over
// cross-cluster connections. Returned by ClusterClient for remote
// clusters. The restricted type enforces the cross-cluster security
// contract at compile time — callers cannot invoke DeletePod,
// UpdatePodState, CreatePod, CountByState, or WatchPods on a
// remote cluster.
type RemotePodOperations interface {
    GetPod(ctx context.Context, podID PodID) (*PodRecord, error)
    ClaimPod(ctx context.Context, opts ClaimOpts) (*PodRecord, error)
    ReleasePod(ctx context.Context, podID PodID, reason ReleaseReason) error
    ListPodsByPool(ctx context.Context, poolID PoolID, filter PodFilter) ([]PodRecord, error)
}

type ClusterRegistry interface {
    ListClusters(ctx context.Context) ([]ClusterInfo, error)
    GetCluster(ctx context.Context, clusterID ClusterID) (*ClusterInfo, error)
    SelectCluster(ctx context.Context, req ClusterSelectionRequest) (ClusterID, error)
    ClusterClient(ctx context.Context, clusterID ClusterID) (RemotePodOperations, error)
    LocalClusterID() ClusterID
}
// v1: LocalClusterRegistry (single cluster, ClaimOpts.ClusterID always nil)
// LocalClusterRegistry.ClusterClient returns the local CRDPodRegistry,
// which satisfies RemotePodOperations (it is a superset).
```

**Cross-cluster transport security contract.** Implementations of `ClusterClient` that return a remote `RemotePodOperations` (i.e., any implementation other than `LocalClusterRegistry`) MUST establish a mutually authenticated TLS connection to the remote cluster's PodRegistry endpoint. Requirements: (1) the connection MUST use mTLS with certificates issued by the platform's cert-manager CA (§10.3) — the client presents a gateway certificate and the server presents a certificate whose SAN matches the endpoint declared in `ClusterInfo`; (2) `ClusterInfo` MUST include a `CACertBundle` field containing the remote cluster's CA certificate chain, used by the client to verify the server's identity — this prevents a compromised cluster from impersonating another via a different CA; (3) the remote PodRegistry endpoint MUST validate that the caller's certificate SAN matches the expected gateway identity of the originating cluster before accepting any operation; (4) operations not in `RemotePodOperations` (`DeletePod`, `UpdatePodState`, `CreatePod`, `CountByState`, `WatchPods`) are prohibited over cross-cluster connections — this is enforced at compile time by the `RemotePodOperations` return type and at runtime by the remote endpoint, which MUST reject any RPC for a non-permitted operation from a cross-cluster caller. The `LocalClusterRegistry` (v1) is exempt — it returns an in-process `PodRegistry` reference (which satisfies `RemotePodOperations`) with no network transport.

**Cross-cluster CA cert bundle rotation.** When a remote cluster rotates its CA (following the overlap procedure in §10.3), the local cluster's `ClusterInfo.CACertBundle` must be updated to include the new CA chain before the old CA expires. During the overlap window, `CACertBundle` MUST contain both the old and new CA certificates so that connections succeed regardless of which CA signed the remote endpoint's current certificate. `ClusterRegistry` implementations that persist `ClusterInfo` (e.g., in Postgres or a ConfigMap) SHOULD support a `UpdateClusterCACertBundle` operation or equivalent; implementations that derive `ClusterInfo` from a shared secret store (e.g., Vault) inherit rotation from the store's update mechanism.

**Cross-cluster `PodRecord` compatibility contract.** Every `PodRegistry` implementation — whether accessed locally or via `ClusterClient` — MUST produce `PodRecord` values that satisfy the following invariants. These are implicit in the current single-implementation design but must be stated explicitly to prevent subtle cross-implementation bugs if clusters run different `PodRegistry` backends (e.g., one cluster on `CRDPodRegistry`, another on a future `PostgresPodRegistry`):

1. **State machine values.** The `State` field MUST be one of the canonical pod state machine values defined in §4.6.1 (`idle`, `claimed`, `running`, `draining`, `terminated`, `failed`). No implementation-specific states are permitted.
2. **`resource_version` monotonicity.** `resource_version` MUST be strictly monotonically increasing for a given `PodID` within a single `PodRegistry` implementation. Cross-implementation comparisons of `resource_version` are undefined — callers MUST NOT compare `resource_version` values obtained from different `PodRegistry` implementations (e.g., a `resource_version` from a `CRDPodRegistry` on cluster A vs. a future `PostgresPodRegistry` on cluster B).
3. **`ClaimPod` atomicity.** `ClaimPod` MUST be atomic with respect to concurrent callers across all access paths (local and remote). A pod claimed via `ClusterClient` on cluster A MUST NOT be concurrently claimable via direct `PodRegistry` access on cluster B. This is enforced by the remote endpoint's own `PodRegistry` implementation — the `ClusterClient` delegates to the remote cluster's `PodRegistry`, which holds the authoritative state.

**Version negotiation.** Future `ClusterClient` implementations SHOULD include a lightweight version handshake on connection establishment: the remote endpoint reports its `PodRegistry` implementation type and schema version (e.g., `crd/v1`, `postgres/v1`) in the gRPC metadata of the first response. The local cluster logs this as a `cluster_registry_peer_version` structured event. No behavioral branching is required — the handshake is diagnostic, enabling operators to detect mixed-version deployments before they cause issues.

#### CredentialGenerator

Abstracts credential acquisition from a pool. The v1 implementation (`StaticPoolGenerator`) wraps the existing `CredentialPool` logic: it selects an available credential from the pool using the existing lease-based allocation path (§4.9).

```go
type CredentialGenerator interface {
    GenerateCredential(ctx context.Context, req CredentialRequest) (*CredentialLease, error)
}
// v1: StaticPoolGenerator (wraps CredentialPool, existing lease allocation)
```

#### EventBus

Abstracts control-plane event distribution for delegation tree signals and session lifecycle events. The v1 implementation (`RedisEventBus`) publishes to Redis pub/sub channels under the tenant-prefix convention (§12.4). If the EventBus backend is later replaced with NATS JetStream or Kafka for scalability, the swap is a configuration-only change at the store-router level — no callers change.

```go
// EventTopic identifies a control-plane event channel.
// Adding a topic requires updating this list and the §12.4 key table.
type EventTopic string

const (
    TopicDelegationTree   EventTopic = "delegation_tree"   // budget signals, child completion, tree cancellation
    TopicSessionLifecycle EventTopic = "session_lifecycle"  // state transitions, coordinator handoff notifications
)

type EventBus interface {
    Publish(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID, topic EventTopic, payload []byte) error
    Subscribe(ctx context.Context, tenantID TenantID, topic EventTopic,
        handler func(context.Context, []byte) error) (Subscription, error)
}
// v1: RedisEventBus (Redis pub/sub, tenant-prefix key convention)
```

**Delivery contract.** EventBus provides **at-most-once** delivery in v1 (Redis pub/sub drops messages when no subscriber is connected). Callers MUST tolerate missed events — any caller that requires guaranteed delivery must also poll durable state (Postgres) as a fallback. If the EventBus backend is later replaced with NATS JetStream or Kafka for scalability, the delivery guarantee strengthens to at-least-once, introducing the possibility of duplicate delivery. Handlers MUST be idempotent to remain correct under either guarantee. See the handler design note below.

**Handler design.** EventBus events are **notifications, not commands.** The payload tells the handler *what changed*; the handler reads durable state (Postgres, Redis) to determine *what to do*. For example, a `TopicDelegationTree` event signaling child completion causes the gateway to read the child's terminal state from `SessionStore` — receiving the same notification twice simply triggers a redundant state check and is a no-op. This convergent design makes handlers naturally idempotent without requiring event IDs, deduplication infrastructure, or exactly-once delivery. Handler errors are logged by the implementation; the v1 `RedisEventBus` does not retry. Future at-least-once implementations SHOULD support configurable retry with dead-letter after exhaustion.

**Delivery-guarantee transition protocol.** Replacing `RedisEventBus` with an at-least-once implementation (NATS JetStream, Kafka) changes runtime delivery behavior for all subscribers. The "notifications not commands" design (above) makes handlers structurally idempotent, but subtle violations can hide in handlers that perform unguarded side effects (e.g., incrementing a metric per event rather than per state change, or logging at a rate that becomes problematic under replay). Before enabling a durable EventBus implementation in production, operators MUST execute the following validation sequence in a staging environment:

1. **Duplicate-injection test.** Configure the staging EventBus to publish every event twice (configurable via `eventBus.duplicateInjectionFactor: 2` test-only Helm value). Run the full integration test suite. Verify that no handler produces doubled side effects — specifically, check that business-metric counters (e.g., delegation tree completion counts) do not correlate with the doubled publish rate.
2. **Redelivery-after-crash test.** Kill a subscriber mid-handler (SIGKILL, not SIGTERM). Verify that the at-least-once implementation redelivers the in-flight event to another subscriber and that the handler converges to the same outcome as a clean first delivery — i.e., the durable state read in the handler returns the same result regardless of whether the prior handler completed.
3. **Metric audit.** Review all `lenny_event_bus_handler_*` metric emissions for counters that increment per event without deduplication. Any counter that is semantically "per-occurrence" (not "per-state-change") must be guarded by a durable-state check or converted to a gauge.

These checks are one-time validation steps during the backend swap, not ongoing CI obligations.

**Tenant isolation.** The `tenantID` parameter is mandatory. The implementation constructs the underlying channel name by prefixing with the tenant ID (e.g., Redis channel `t:{tenant_id}:evt:{topic}`). Callers never construct raw channel names. This ensures tenant isolation is enforced at the interface boundary, not by caller convention. The `t:{tenant_id}:evt:{topic}` channels follow the §12.4 tenant-prefix key convention and must be added to the canonical key table and to `TestRedisTenantKeyIsolation` coverage.

**Future: partition key for per-tree subscriber filtering.** At scale, a gateway replica coordinating one root session receives all `TopicDelegationTree` events for the entire tenant — wasteful when the tenant has many concurrent trees. A future interface revision can add an optional `partitionKey string` parameter to `Publish` and `Subscribe`. When set (e.g., to `root_session_id`), the implementation appends it to the channel name (`t:{tenant_id}:evt:delegation_tree:{root_session_id}`), so each subscriber receives only events for the trees it coordinates. When empty, the channel is tenant-wide (current behavior). In Redis Cluster, the `{tenant_id}` hash tag is still the first `{...}` match, so all EventBus channels for a tenant remain on the same shard — the partition key provides subscriber filtering, not hash-slot distribution. In NATS, the partition key maps to a hierarchical subject segment with wildcard subscribe available for monitoring; in Kafka, it becomes the partition key for both filtering and distribution. This change is additive — it does not require modifying existing callers (they pass `""`) and is compatible with the §12.4 key prefix convention.

**Observability contract.** Every `EventBus` implementation MUST emit: `lenny_event_bus_publish_total{topic}` (counter), `lenny_event_bus_publish_duration_seconds{topic}` (histogram), `lenny_event_bus_handler_duration_seconds{topic}` (histogram), and `lenny_event_bus_handler_error_total{topic}` (counter). The `topic` label is the `EventTopic` constant value (`delegation_tree`, `session_lifecycle`). Handler duration measures time spent in the caller-supplied handler function, not the pub/sub transport. These metrics enable operators to detect silent publish failures, slow handlers, and handler error accumulation — particularly important when diagnosing delegation tree signal delays or missed session lifecycle events.

**Infrastructure pub/sub exclusion.** Three pub/sub pathways in the system intentionally bypass EventBus: circuit breaker state propagation (§11.6, channel `circuit_breaker_events`), credential deny list propagation (§4.9), and certificate deny list propagation (§10.3). These are **gateway-internal replica synchronization** mechanisms that share three properties: (1) they are platform-wide, not tenant-scoped; (2) they use a poll-with-pub/sub-hint pattern where the pub/sub channel is a latency optimization, not a delivery guarantee (each has a polling fallback and startup-read path); (3) they are low-volume and do not benefit from a future NATS/Kafka migration. Routing these through EventBus would require either a nil `TenantID` (undermining the isolation contract) or a separate method signature (complicating the interface for no operational benefit).

**Build phase assignments.** The following table maps each §12.6 interface to the build phase (§18) that introduces it and its earliest consumer. Implementations are delivered in the listed phase; the interface definition (Go `type ... interface`) may be committed earlier as a compile target.

| Interface | Phase | Earliest consumer | v1 implementation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `PodRegistry` | 3 | `PoolScalingController` (`CountByState`, `WatchPods`); session manager `ClaimPod` in Phase 4 | `CRDPodRegistry` (Kubernetes API) |
| `StoreRouter` | 4 | Session manager billing/audit writes (R-03 requires routing through `StoreRouter`) | `SingleShardRouter` (single Postgres pool) |
| `CredentialGenerator` | 5.5 | Basic credential leasing (`AssignCredentials` RPC) | `StaticPoolGenerator` (wraps `CredentialPool`) |
| `ClusterRegistry` | 9 | `lenny/delegate_task` cross-cluster routing; v1 `LocalClusterRegistry` is trivial | `LocalClusterRegistry` (single cluster) |
| `EventBus` | 9 | Delegation tree signals (`TopicDelegationTree`); session lifecycle notifications | `RedisEventBus` (Redis pub/sub) |

### 12.7 Extensibility

- Define small role-based interfaces
- Start with Postgres + Redis
- Keep migrations/schema explicit and backend-specific
- Add new backends only under real pressure
- Token store module is separate even if initially backed by Postgres (allows future Vault/KMS migration)

### 12.8 Compliance Interfaces

**T4 ephemeral workspace isolation.** Node-level disk encryption (Section 6.4) does not provide per-tenant key isolation for T4 workspace data on emptyDir — a node compromise exposes all T4 tenants co-located on that node. To enforce per-tenant key isolation at the node boundary, T4 workloads are required to run on dedicated node pools (Section 6.4 T4 dedicated-node requirement). The `lenny-t4-node-isolation` validating webhook (Section 6.4) enforces this at admission time and is fail-closed. This control is in addition to the SSE-KMS per-tenant key requirement for MinIO artifacts (Section 12.5), which covers durable (post-checkpoint) workspace data.

**Legal hold.** Sessions and artifacts support a `legal_hold` boolean flag. When set, the artifact retention policy is suspended — artifacts are not deleted by the GC job regardless of TTL. Legal holds are set and cleared via the admin API (`POST /v1/admin/legal-hold`), which accepts a session ID or artifact ID and the desired hold state.

**Legal hold and checkpoint rotation.** When `legal_hold` is set on a session or artifact, **all** retention rotation policies for that session are suspended — the GC job skips both TTL-based deletion and the "latest 2 checkpoints" rotation for any session whose `legal_hold` flag is `true`. Checkpoints accumulate until the hold is lifted; only then does the normal rotation policy resume and trim to the "latest 2" limit. This prevents checkpoint spoliation — discarding intermediate forensic state while a hold is in effect constitutes destruction of potentially discoverable evidence. Storage growth for long-held sessions is accepted as a compliance cost; operators should monitor `lenny_checkpoint_storage_bytes_total` (gauge, labeled by `tenant_id` and `pool`) and the `CheckpointStorageHigh` alert (Section 16.5), and should allocate additional quota for tenants with active holds.

**Legal hold reconciler.** A background reconciler (co-located with the GC goroutine, running every 15 minutes) scans for sessions where `legal_hold = true` and one or more checkpoints have already been rotated (i.e., there are gaps in the checkpoint sequence recorded in the `artifact_store` table). When such a session is found, the reconciler emits a `legal_hold.checkpoint_gap_detected` critical audit event and increments the `lenny_legal_hold_checkpoint_gaps_total` counter. This surfaces any pre-existing rotation that occurred before the hold was set so that compliance and legal teams can assess the impact. The reconciler does not attempt to recover deleted checkpoints — it provides detection and audit trail only.

**Data erasure (GDPR).** Each store interface includes `DeleteByUser(user_id)` and `DeleteByTenant(tenant_id)` methods. Erasure is implemented as a background job that runs to completion and produces an erasure receipt stored in the audit trail.

**User-level erasure invocation.** User-level erasure is triggered via the Admin API: `POST /v1/admin/users/{user_id}/erase` initiates the background erasure job and returns a job ID. The job status is queryable via `GET /v1/admin/erasure-jobs/{job_id}`, which returns the current phase, completion percentage, time elapsed, and any errors. These endpoints are listed in Section 15.1.

**Erasure job phase field (crash recovery).** Each erasure job record persists a `phase` field (enum: `initiated | store_deleting | pseudonymizing | verifying | completed | failed`) in Postgres. The phase is updated atomically with each step transition. If the controller crashes and restarts, it reads the persisted `phase` to determine the safe resume point: jobs in `store_deleting` re-run `DeleteByUser` (all store deletions are idempotent); jobs in `pseudonymizing` check whether the billing pseudonymization transaction already committed before re-applying; jobs in `verifying` re-run the salt-deletion verification check. This prevents double-deletion errors and ensures crash recovery cannot silently skip a deletion step.

**Processing restriction during erasure (GDPR Article 18).** When an erasure job is initiated, the platform sets `processing_restricted: true` on the user record in the `UserStore`. While this flag is set: (1) all session creation endpoints (`POST /v1/sessions` and `POST /v1/sessions/start`) are rejected with `403 ERASURE_IN_PROGRESS` for the affected `user_id`; (2) the error body includes `{"code": "ERASURE_IN_PROGRESS", "userId": "<user_id>", "jobId": "<job_id>", "message": "This user has a pending erasure request.
